title: Ekahau Wireless Survey Japan: Professional WiFi Assessment and Design
excerpt: Professional Ekahau wireless surveys for Tokyo offices. Learn about predictive vs. active surveys, WiFi heatmaps, channel planning, and when to hire a professional wireless surveyor.
htmlContent:
Ekahau Wireless Survey Japan: Professional WiFi Assessment and Design

When a 120-person professional services firm moved into their new Tokyo office in Minato-ku in 2025, they assumed their WiFi would "just work." They installed the same access point model their IT director had used successfully at his previous company, placing one AP in each corner of the floor and one in the center. The result was predictable: conference rooms had dead zones where video calls dropped, the open office area suffered from channel interference that slowed speeds to a crawl, and the executive suite—located in a corner surrounded by glass walls—had virtually no coverage at all. Employees resorted to using mobile hotspots, and the IT helpdesk was flooded with complaints. After six months of frustration, they hired a professional to conduct an Ekahau wireless survey. The survey revealed that their AP placement provided adequate coverage in only 40% of the office, channel overlap was creating interference on 60% of their spectrum, and their power levels were set incorrectly for the office's density. A redesign based on the survey results cost ¥800,000—but it eliminated the WiFi problems that had been costing them an estimated ¥2 million annually in lost productivity.

This story illustrates why professional wireless surveys matter. WiFi is invisible and unpredictable—what looks like adequate coverage on paper often fails in practice. This guide explains what Ekahau wireless surveys are, when you need one, and how professional WiFi assessment can save your organization from costly WiFi problems.

What Is an Ekahau Wireless Survey?

Ekahau is the industry-leading tool for wireless network design, analysis, and troubleshooting. An Ekahau survey provides detailed, data-driven analysis of WiFi coverage, performance, and interference.

Predictive vs. Active vs. Passive Surveys

Ekahau supports three types of surveys, each serving different purposes:

Predictive surveys are conducted before any equipment is installed. Using Ekahau's software, a surveyor creates a digital model of your space—including wall materials, floor plans, and expected usage—and simulates how WiFi signals will propagate. The software predicts coverage, identifies potential problem areas, and recommends optimal access point placement.

Predictive surveys are ideal for new construction or major renovations where you need to plan WiFi infrastructure before installation. They're faster and less expensive than physical surveys but rely on accurate input data. If the floor plan doesn't reflect reality—if walls are a different material than specified, for example—the predictions may be inaccurate.

Active surveys involve walking through the space with a laptop running Ekahau software while connected to the WiFi network. The software measures actual signal strength, throughput, and performance at each location. Active surveys show you what users actually experience when connected to the network.

Active surveys are ideal for troubleshooting existing networks or validating that a new installation meets requirements. They provide real-world performance data but require a functioning network to survey.

Passive surveys involve walking through the space with Ekahau software listening to all WiFi signals in the environment—not just your network. The software maps signal strength from all access points, identifies interference sources, and analyzes channel utilization.

Passive surveys are ideal for understanding the RF environment, identifying interference sources, and planning channel assignments. They don't require connecting to a network and can be conducted even before your network is installed.

WiFi Heatmaps and What They Show

Ekahau produces visual heatmaps that make WiFi performance visible:

Signal strength heatmaps show WiFi signal strength throughout your space. Strong signals are shown in green and blue; weak signals in yellow and red. These maps immediately reveal coverage gaps and areas where signal is too weak for reliable connectivity.

Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) heatmaps show the quality of the WiFi signal relative to background noise. Even strong signals can be unusable if noise levels are high. SNR maps identify areas where interference is degrading performance.

Throughput prediction heatmaps estimate the data rates users can expect throughout the space. These maps account for signal strength, interference, and device capabilities to predict actual performance.

Channel overlap maps show where access points on the same or overlapping channels interfere with each other. Channel overlap is a common cause of WiFi performance problems.

Secondary coverage maps show whether you have adequate backup coverage if an access point fails. Redundancy is important for business-critical WiFi.

Channel Planning and Spectrum Analysis

Ekahau provides detailed spectrum analysis:

Channel utilization shows how heavily each WiFi channel is used. In crowded environments like Tokyo, channel selection is critical for performance.

Non-WiFi interference identifies interference from non-WiFi sources like microwave ovens, Bluetooth devices, and industrial equipment. These sources can severely impact WiFi performance.

Channel recommendations suggest optimal channel assignments for your access points to minimize interference and maximize performance.

DFS channel analysis examines Dynamic Frequency Selection channels that are available but may be subject to radar detection requirements. DFS channels offer more spectrum but require careful management.

When Do You Need a Professional Wireless Survey?

Not every WiFi deployment requires a professional survey, but many benefit from one.

New Office or Renovation Planning

If you're building a new office or renovating an existing space, a predictive survey should be part of your planning:

Before construction: A predictive survey during the design phase ensures that network infrastructure is planned correctly from the start. It's much easier to run cable and mount access points during construction than after.

During construction: As construction progresses, conditions may change. A follow-up survey can validate that the design remains appropriate.

Before occupancy: A final active survey before employees move in validates that the installed network meets requirements.

The cost of a predictive survey (typically ¥150,000-300,000 for a medium-sized office) is small compared to the cost of fixing WiFi problems after occupancy.

Existing WiFi Problems

If you're experiencing WiFi problems, a survey can identify the root cause:

Coverage gaps: Users report dead zones where WiFi doesn't work. A survey maps coverage and identifies gaps.

Slow speeds: WiFi is slow even when signal strength appears good. A survey can identify interference, channel overlap, or configuration issues.

Unreliable connections: Connections drop frequently or devices struggle to connect. A survey can identify roaming issues, interference, or capacity problems.

Capacity problems: WiFi works for a few users but degrades when many users are active. A survey can identify capacity limitations and recommend solutions.

Pre-Purchase Validation

Before investing in new WiFi equipment, a survey can validate your plans:

Vendor evaluation: Different vendors' equipment performs differently. A survey can compare options in your actual environment.

AP quantity validation: Vendors may recommend a certain number of access points, but is that right for your space? A survey can validate or adjust the recommendation.

Budget justification: A survey provides data to justify WiFi investment. If the survey shows problems, it helps build the case for funding.

Compliance and Audit Requirements

Some organizations require WiFi surveys for compliance:

Regulatory compliance: Some regulations require documented WiFi coverage for safety or operational reasons.

Insurance requirements: Some cyber insurance policies require documented network assessments.

Audit preparation: For organizations subject to IT audits, a WiFi survey demonstrates due diligence in network design.

The Ekahau Survey Process

Understanding the survey process helps you prepare and evaluate results.

Pre-Survey Planning and Information Gathering

Before conducting a survey, the surveyor gathers information:

Floor plans: Accurate floor plans are essential. CAD files are preferred, but PDFs or even hand-drawn plans can work if dimensions are accurate.

Wall materials: WiFi signals are affected by wall materials. Concrete blocks signals more than drywall; metal reflects signals. The surveyor needs to know what walls are made of.

Current infrastructure: If there's existing WiFi, the surveyor needs to know what's installed—access point models, locations, and configurations.

Usage requirements: How many users? What applications? What performance is needed? A guest network has different requirements than a network supporting video conferencing.

Constraints: Are there areas where APs can't be installed? Aesthetic requirements? Budget limitations?

On-Site Survey Execution

The actual survey involves walking the space:

Predictive survey: The surveyor builds a digital model of the space in Ekahau software, specifying wall materials, floor plans, and other environmental factors. The software then simulates WiFi propagation.

Active/passive survey: The surveyor walks through the space with a laptop running Ekahau software, following a systematic path to cover the entire area. For a medium-sized office, this might take 2-4 hours.

Calibration: The surveyor may take calibration measurements to validate the model or verify equipment performance.

Documentation: The surveyor documents access point locations, cable runs, and other infrastructure details.

Data Analysis and Reporting

After the survey, the surveyor analyzes data and produces reports:

Heatmap generation: Ekahau generates heatmaps showing coverage, performance, and interference.

Problem identification: The surveyor identifies problem areas—coverage gaps, interference sources, capacity limitations.

Recommendations: Based on findings, the surveyor provides recommendations for AP placement, configuration, and channel planning.

Report production: The surveyor produces a formal report documenting findings, analysis, and recommendations.

Recommendation Implementation

After receiving the survey report, you implement recommendations:

AP placement: Install or relocate access points according to survey recommendations.

Configuration changes: Adjust power levels, channels, and other settings as recommended.

Validation: After implementation, conduct a follow-up survey to validate that problems are resolved.

Tokyo-Specific WiFi Challenges

Tokyo's environment creates specific WiFi challenges.

High-Density Environments

Tokyo offices are often high-density:

Many users per area: Open office designs can pack many users into a small area. Each user may have multiple devices (laptop, phone, tablet).

Device density: High device density creates contention for airtime. Even if each device uses little bandwidth, the aggregate demand can overwhelm the network.

Capacity planning: High-density environments require careful capacity planning. More access points may be needed, but they must be carefully configured to avoid interference.

WiFi 6 benefits: WiFi 6 (802.11ax) handles high-density environments better than previous standards. Consider WiFi 6 for new deployments in dense environments.

Interference from Neighboring Networks

Tokyo's density creates interference challenges:

Many neighboring networks: In a typical Tokyo office building, you might see 20-50 neighboring WiFi networks. These networks compete for the same spectrum.

Limited spectrum: The 2.4 GHz band has only three non-overlapping channels. In dense environments, these channels are often completely saturated.

5 GHz advantages: The 5 GHz band offers more channels and less congestion. Design your network to use 5 GHz as much as possible.

Channel planning: Careful channel planning is essential in dense environments. A survey can identify the least congested channels.

Building Materials and Construction Types

Japanese buildings use materials that affect WiFi:

Concrete construction: Many Tokyo buildings use concrete extensively. Concrete blocks WiFi signals effectively, creating coverage challenges.

Metal structures: Elevator shafts, HVAC systems, and metal studs can reflect or block WiFi signals.

Glass: Modern buildings often use extensive glass. While glass doesn't block WiFi as much as concrete, low-emissivity (low-E) glass can reflect signals.

Traditional materials: Older buildings may use traditional Japanese materials like shoji screens and wood. These affect WiFi differently than modern materials.

Regulatory Compliance in Japan

Japan has specific WiFi regulations:

Frequency bands: Japan allows different frequency bands than other countries. Ensure your equipment is certified for use in Japan.

Power limits: Japan has specific power limits for WiFi transmitters. Equipment must be configured to comply with these limits.

DFS requirements: Some 5 GHz channels require Dynamic Frequency Selection (DFS) to avoid interference with radar. DFS channels offer more spectrum but require careful management.

Certification: WiFi equipment used in Japan must have technical conformity certification. Using uncertified equipment is illegal.

DIY vs. Professional Wireless Surveys

Should you conduct your own survey or hire a professional?

When DIY Is Sufficient

DIY surveys may be sufficient for:

Small spaces: A small office with simple requirements may not need professional analysis.

Basic validation: If you've installed equipment and just want to validate that it works, free tools may suffice.

Troubleshooting simple problems: For obvious problems like a failed access point, professional analysis may be overkill.

Budget constraints: If budget is extremely limited, DIY is better than no survey.

When to Hire a Professional

Professional surveys are warranted for:

Business-critical WiFi: If WiFi is essential to your business operations, professional design is justified.

Large or complex spaces: Offices over 500 square meters, multi-floor deployments, or spaces with complex layouts benefit from professional analysis.

High-density environments: Dense environments require careful capacity planning that benefits from professional expertise.

Performance problems: If you're experiencing WiFi problems you can't solve, professional analysis can identify root causes.

New construction: For new construction, professional design during the planning phase prevents costly problems later.

Cost-Benefit Analysis

Consider the costs and benefits:

Professional survey cost: ¥150,000-500,000 depending on space size and complexity.

Cost of WiFi problems: Lost productivity, support tickets, user frustration, and potential business impact. For a 50-person office, WiFi problems can easily cost ¥1-2 million annually.

Cost of rework: Fixing WiFi problems after installation often costs more than doing it right the first time. Re-cabling, additional access points, and IT labor add up quickly.

For most business environments, professional surveys are cost-effective insurance against WiFi problems.

Selecting a Wireless Survey Provider in Tokyo

If you decide to hire a professional, choose carefully.

Certifications and Experience to Look For

Evaluate provider qualifications:

Ekahau certification: Look for Ekahau Certified Survey Engineers (ECSE) certification. This demonstrates training on the Ekahau platform.

Vendor certifications: Certifications from major WiFi vendors (Cisco, Aruba, etc.) demonstrate technical knowledge.

Experience: Ask about experience with similar projects. Have they surveyed offices like yours? Do they understand your industry's requirements?

References: Ask for references from similar projects. Contact references to verify satisfaction.

Questions to Ask Potential Surveyors

Interview potential providers:

"What type of survey do you recommend for my situation—predictive, active, or passive?"

"What deliverables will I receive? Will I get heatmaps, a written report, and recommendations?"

"How long will the survey take, and what's your availability?"

"Do you also implement recommendations, or do you only provide analysis?"

"What's your experience with high-density environments like mine?"

"Can you provide references from similar projects?"

Deliverables You Should Receive

Expect comprehensive deliverables:

Heatmaps: Visual maps showing coverage, performance, and interference.

Written report: Document explaining findings, analysis, and recommendations.

AP placement recommendations: Specific guidance on where to place access points.

Configuration recommendations: Guidance on power levels, channels, and other settings.

Bill of materials: If new equipment is needed, a list of recommended equipment.

Implementation guidance: Advice on implementing recommendations.

Post-Survey Support Options

Consider ongoing support:

Implementation support: Will the surveyor help implement recommendations? This can be valuable if you don't have in-house WiFi expertise.

Validation survey: Will the surveyor conduct a follow-up survey after implementation to validate results?

Ongoing support: Is the surveyor available for questions or troubleshooting after the project?

Maintenance surveys: Will the surveyor conduct periodic surveys to ensure continued performance?

Common WiFi Problems an Ekahau Survey Can Identify

Surveys reveal problems that aren't visible otherwise.

Coverage Gaps and Dead Zones

Coverage gaps occur where signal is too weak:

Causes: Insufficient access points, APs placed incorrectly, building materials blocking signals, or APs configured with too low power.

Impact: Users can't connect or experience frequent disconnections in affected areas.

Survey identification: Signal strength heatmaps show coverage gaps as red or yellow areas.

Solutions: Add access points, relocate existing APs, increase power levels, or add directional antennas.

Channel Interference and Overlap

Interference occurs when access points interfere with each other:

Causes: Too many APs on the same or overlapping channels, neighboring networks using the same channels, or non-WiFi interference sources.

Impact: Slow speeds, unreliable connections, and poor performance even when signal strength is good.

Survey identification: Channel overlap maps and spectrum analysis show interference.

Solutions: Reconfigure channels, reduce power levels to limit overlap, or add access points to reduce the coverage area of each AP.

Capacity Limitations

Capacity problems occur when the network can't handle the number of users:

Causes: Too few access points for the number of users, outdated WiFi standards, or insufficient backhaul bandwidth.

Impact: Slow speeds when many users are active, difficulty connecting during peak times.

Survey identification: Throughput predictions and user density analysis reveal capacity limitations.

Solutions: Add access points, upgrade to WiFi 6, increase backhaul bandwidth, or implement load balancing.

Roaming Problems

Roaming problems occur when devices don't switch between access points smoothly:

Causes: Incorrect roaming thresholds, insufficient overlap between AP coverage areas, or configuration issues.

Impact: Connections drop when users move between areas, or devices stick to distant APs instead of switching to closer ones.

Survey identification: Analysis of coverage overlap and roaming behavior reveals problems.

Solutions: Adjust roaming thresholds, tune power levels to create appropriate overlap, or configure roaming features properly.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing professional Ekahau wireless surveys for international companies operating in Japan. Our certified survey engineers conduct predictive, active, and passive surveys to design, troubleshoot, and optimize WiFi networks in Tokyo's challenging RF environment. From small offices to large enterprise deployments, we provide the data-driven analysis you need for reliable WiFi performance. Contact us to schedule your wireless survey.

---

Related Articles

  • WiFi Assessment Tokyo: Diagnosing and Fixing Enterprise Wireless
  • Enterprise WiFi Japan: Design Standards for High-Performance Networks
  • Wireless Site Survey Tokyo: When, Why, and How to Assess Your WiFi

Configuration recommendations: Guidance on power levels, channels, and other settings.

Bill of materials: If new equipment is needed, a list of recommended equipment.

Implementation guidance: Advice on implementing recommendations.

Post-Survey Support Options

Consider ongoing support:

Implementation support: Will the surveyor help implement recommendations? This can be valuable if you don't have in-house WiFi expertise.

Validation survey: Will the surveyor conduct a follow-up survey after implementation to validate results?

Ongoing support: Is the surveyor available for questions or troubleshooting after the project?

Maintenance surveys: Will the surveyor conduct periodic surveys to ensure continued performance?

Common WiFi Problems an Ekahau Survey Can Identify

Surveys reveal problems that aren't visible otherwise.

Coverage Gaps and Dead Zones

Coverage gaps occur where signal is too weak:

Causes: Insufficient access points, APs placed incorrectly, building materials blocking signals, or APs configured with too low power.

Impact: Users can't connect or experience frequent disconnections in affected areas.

Survey identification: Signal strength heatmaps show coverage gaps as red or yellow areas.

Solutions: Add access points, relocate existing APs, increase power levels, or add directional antennas.

Channel Interference and Overlap

Interference occurs when access points interfere with each other:

Causes: Too many APs on the same or overlapping channels, neighboring networks using the same channels, or non-WiFi interference sources.

Impact: Slow speeds, unreliable connections, and poor performance even when signal strength is good.

Survey identification: Channel overlap maps and spectrum analysis show interference.

Solutions: Reconfigure channels, reduce power levels to limit overlap, or add access points to reduce the coverage area of each AP.

Capacity Limitations

Capacity problems occur when the network can't handle the number of users:

Causes: Too few access points for the number of users, outdated WiFi standards, or insufficient backhaul bandwidth.

Impact: Slow speeds when many users are active, difficulty connecting during peak times.

Survey identification: Throughput predictions and user density analysis reveal capacity limitations.

Solutions: Add access points, upgrade to WiFi 6, increase backhaul bandwidth, or implement load balancing.

Roaming Problems

Roaming problems occur when devices don't switch between access points smoothly:

Causes: Incorrect roaming thresholds, insufficient overlap between AP coverage areas, or configuration issues.

Impact: Connections drop when users move between areas, or devices stick to distant APs instead of switching to closer ones.

Survey identification: Analysis of coverage overlap and roaming behavior reveals problems.

Solutions: Adjust roaming thresholds, tune power levels to create appropriate overlap, or configure roaming features properly.

---

Our certified survey engineers conduct predictive, active, and passive surveys to design, troubleshoot, and optimize WiFi networks in Tokyo's challenging RF environment. From small offices to large enterprise deployments, we provide the data-driven analysis you need for reliable WiFi performance. Contact us to schedule your wireless survey.

title: WiFi Assessment Tokyo: Diagnosing and Fixing Enterprise Wireless Problems
excerpt: Diagnose and fix WiFi problems in Tokyo offices. Learn about common issues, troubleshooting methods, performance testing, and when to call a professional for wireless assessment.
htmlContent:
WiFi Assessment Tokyo: Diagnosing and Fixing Enterprise Wireless Problems

The head of IT at a 200-person marketing agency in Shibuya was frustrated. For months, employees had been complaining about WiFi problems—slow speeds, dropped connections, and dead zones in conference rooms. The IT team had tried everything they could think of: rebooting access points, changing channels, even adding more APs. But the problems persisted. Some users had resorted to using mobile hotspots for critical work. The final straw came when a major client presentation was disrupted by a video call that kept dropping due to WiFi issues. The agency's reputation was at stake. The IT director finally called in a professional to conduct a comprehensive WiFi assessment. Within a day, the assessment revealed the root causes: the additional APs they had installed were actually making interference worse, power levels were set incorrectly causing roaming problems, and the conference room dead zones were caused by a recently installed metal whiteboard that blocked signals. With targeted fixes based on the assessment, the WiFi problems were resolved within a week. The cost of the assessment and fixes: ¥400,000. The cost of continuing to struggle with WiFi problems: immeasurable in lost productivity and damaged reputation.

This story illustrates a common pattern: organizations struggle with WiFi problems for months or years, trying various fixes without systematic diagnosis, when a professional assessment could identify and resolve issues quickly. This guide explains how to diagnose WiFi problems, what tools and methods are available, and when to call in professional help.

Common WiFi Problems in Tokyo Offices

Understanding common problems helps focus troubleshooting efforts.

Slow Speeds Despite Good Signal Strength

Users report that WiFi is slow even when they show full signal bars:

Possible causes:

  • Channel interference from neighboring networks
  • Too many users sharing limited bandwidth
  • Legacy WiFi standards (802.11n or older)
  • Backhaul bandwidth limitations
  • QoS misconfiguration

Diagnostic approach:

  • Check actual throughput with speed tests
  • Analyze channel utilization
  • Verify what WiFi standard is being used
  • Check backhaul connection speeds
  • Review QoS configuration

Solutions:

  • Optimize channel selection
  • Add access points to increase capacity
  • Upgrade to WiFi 6 equipment
  • Increase backhaul bandwidth
  • Configure QoS properly

Dead Zones and Coverage Gaps

Some areas have no WiFi coverage at all:

Possible causes:

  • Insufficient access point density
  • Building materials blocking signals (concrete, metal)
  • Access point failures
  • Incorrect AP placement
  • Power levels set too low

Diagnostic approach:

  • Map signal strength throughout the space
  • Identify building materials in problem areas
  • Verify all APs are operational
  • Check power level settings

Solutions:

  • Add access points in dead zones
  • Relocate existing APs for better coverage
  • Increase power levels (within regulatory limits)
  • Use directional antennas for targeted coverage

Frequent Disconnections and Dropped Connections

Users are constantly losing their WiFi connection:

Possible causes:

  • Roaming configuration problems
  • Interference causing connection instability
  • Power save mode issues
  • Client device problems
  • Access point overload

Diagnostic approach:

  • Monitor connection logs
  • Check roaming thresholds
  • Analyze interference levels
  • Test with different client devices
  • Check AP load levels

Solutions:

  • Tune roaming parameters
  • Reduce interference through channel optimization
  • Adjust power save settings
  • Upgrade problematic client devices
  • Add capacity to overloaded APs

Video Call and Real-Time Application Issues

Video calls stutter, freeze, or drop:

Possible causes:

  • Insufficient bandwidth for real-time traffic
  • High latency or jitter
  • Packet loss due to interference
  • QoS not prioritizing real-time traffic
  • Insufficient upload bandwidth

Diagnostic approach:

  • Measure latency and jitter
  • Test packet loss rates
  • Check QoS configuration
  • Verify upload bandwidth
  • Analyze traffic patterns during calls

Solutions:

  • Implement QoS for real-time traffic
  • Increase bandwidth if needed
  • Optimize WiFi for low latency
  • Use 5 GHz band for video traffic
  • Consider dedicated video conferencing infrastructure

Roaming Problems Between Access Points

Devices don't switch between APs smoothly:

Possible causes:

  • Insufficient overlap between AP coverage areas
  • Roaming thresholds set incorrectly
  • Client device roaming behavior
  • Different SSID configurations
  • Power levels too high or too low

Diagnostic approach:

  • Map coverage overlap between APs
  • Check roaming threshold settings
  • Test with different client devices
  • Verify SSID configuration consistency
  • Analyze power level settings

Solutions:

  • Adjust power levels to create proper overlap
  • Tune roaming thresholds
  • Ensure consistent SSID configuration
  • Use enterprise-grade clients with better roaming
  • Consider WiFi 6 with improved roaming features

WiFi Assessment Methods and Tools

Various methods and tools can diagnose WiFi problems.

Free WiFi Analyzer Tools

Several free tools provide basic WiFi analysis:

WiFi Analyzer (Windows/Android): Shows signal strength, channel usage, and neighboring networks. Good for quick channel analysis.

inSSIDer (Windows/Mac): More advanced tool showing detailed network information, channel overlap, and signal strength over time.

NetSpot (Windows/Mac): Offers both active and passive surveys with heatmap generation. Free version has limitations.

Acrylic WiFi (Windows): Professional-grade tool with detailed analysis capabilities. Free version available.

Airport Utility (iOS): Basic WiFi scanning built into iOS. Limited but useful for quick checks.

These tools are good for initial diagnosis but have limitations for comprehensive enterprise assessment.

Professional WiFi Survey Tools

Professional tools provide comprehensive analysis:

Ekahau: Industry-leading tool for wireless surveys. Supports predictive, active, and passive surveys with detailed heatmaps and analysis. Used by professional surveyors.

AirMagnet: Comprehensive WiFi analysis and troubleshooting. Good for security analysis as well as performance.

iBwave Wi-Fi: Specialized for enterprise WiFi design and analysis.

These tools require training and experience to use effectively. They're typically used by professional surveyors.

Performance Testing Methods

Measure actual WiFi performance:

Speed tests: Tools like Speedtest.net or iPerf measure actual throughput. Test from multiple locations at different times.

Latency testing: Ping tests measure round-trip time. Consistent low latency is important for real-time applications.

Jitter measurement: Variation in latency affects real-time applications. Tools like iPerf can measure jitter.

Packet loss testing: Lost packets indicate interference or connectivity problems. Monitor packet loss rates.

Load testing: Test performance under load with multiple users or devices active simultaneously.

Spectrum Analysis

Analyze the RF environment:

WiFi spectrum analyzers: Tools like Wi-Spy or professional spectrum analyzers show non-WiFi interference sources.

Common interference sources: Microwave ovens, Bluetooth devices, cordless phones, and industrial equipment can interfere with WiFi.

DFS radar detection: Some 5 GHz channels may be affected by radar. Spectrum analysis can identify these issues.

Client Device Analysis

Client devices affect WiFi experience:

Device capabilities: Different devices support different WiFi standards. Older devices may limit network performance.

Driver issues: Outdated or buggy WiFi drivers can cause connectivity problems.

Power save modes: Aggressive power save modes can cause connectivity issues.

Roam sensitivity: Different devices have different roaming behaviors. Some stick to distant APs too long.

DIY WiFi Troubleshooting Steps

Before calling a professional, try these troubleshooting steps.

Step 1: Document the Problem

Understand what's happening:

When does it occur? Specific times of day? During specific activities? In specific locations?

Who is affected? All users or specific users? Specific device types?

What are the symptoms? Slow speeds? Disconnections? Dead zones? Application-specific issues?

When did it start? Recent changes to the network or environment?

Good documentation helps identify patterns and potential causes.

Step 2: Check Access Point Health

Verify APs are functioning:

Physical inspection: Check that all APs are powered on and showing normal status lights.

Management interface: Log into AP management interface to check for errors or alerts.

Client counts: Check how many clients are connected to each AP. Overloaded APs may need load balancing.

Uptime: Check AP uptime. Recent reboots may indicate problems.

Step 3: Analyze Channel Usage

Check for channel interference:

Scan for neighboring networks: Use WiFi analyzer to see what channels neighboring networks are using.

Check your channel assignments: Verify your APs are on non-overlapping channels.

Identify interference: Look for high channel utilization that may indicate interference.

Consider DFS channels: If 2.4 GHz is crowded, consider using 5 GHz DFS channels.

Step 4: Test from Multiple Locations

Verify coverage throughout the space:

Systematic testing: Test from multiple locations using consistent methods.

Different times: Test at different times of day to identify time-based patterns.

Different devices: Test with different devices to identify device-specific issues.

Document results: Record signal strength, speed test results, and any issues observed.

Step 5: Review Configuration

Check for configuration problems:

Power levels: Verify power levels are appropriate. Too high causes interference; too low causes coverage gaps.

SSID configuration: Ensure SSIDs are configured consistently across APs.

Security settings: Verify security settings are correct and consistent.

QoS configuration: Check that QoS is configured to prioritize important traffic.

Firmware versions: Ensure APs are running current firmware.

Step 6: Check Backhaul Connections

Verify the network behind the WiFi:

Switch connections: Verify APs are properly connected to switches.

Uplink capacity: Check that uplinks have adequate bandwidth.

Switch health: Verify switches are healthy and not overloaded.

Internet connection: Test internet connectivity and speed.

When to Call a Professional

Some situations warrant professional help.

Signs You Need Professional Assessment

Consider professional help if:

Problems persist after DIY efforts: If you've tried troubleshooting and problems continue, professional analysis may identify issues you missed.

Business-critical WiFi is failing: If WiFi problems are affecting business operations, professional help is justified.

Planning major changes: If you're adding significant capacity or changing WiFi infrastructure, professional design prevents problems.

New construction or renovation: For new spaces, professional design during planning prevents costly rework.

Complex environments: High-density environments, multi-floor buildings, or spaces with challenging RF environments benefit from professional analysis.

What a Professional Assessment Includes

Professional assessments are comprehensive:

Site survey: Physical survey of the space including coverage mapping, interference analysis, and capacity assessment.

Heatmaps: Visual maps showing coverage, performance, and interference throughout the space.

Detailed analysis: Root cause analysis of identified problems.

Recommendations: Specific recommendations for fixes, including equipment, configuration, and placement.

Implementation support: Many surveyors can also implement recommendations.

Cost-Benefit of Professional Assessment

Consider the value:

Assessment cost: ¥150,000-400,000 depending on space size and complexity.

Cost of WiFi problems: Lost productivity, support tickets, user frustration, and potential business impact. For a 50-person office, this can easily exceed ¥1 million annually.

Cost of wrong fixes: Implementing the wrong solutions wastes money and may make problems worse.

Value of right fixes: Professional assessment identifies the right fixes the first time, saving time and money.

For most business environments, professional assessments are cost-effective.

Tokyo-Specific WiFi Troubleshooting Considerations

Tokyo's environment creates specific challenges.

High-Density Interference

Tokyo's density creates interference challenges:

Many neighboring networks: Expect to see 20-50 neighboring networks in a typical office building. This creates channel congestion.

5 GHz preference: Use 5 GHz as much as possible. It has more channels and less congestion than 2.4 GHz.

Channel planning: Careful channel planning is essential. Professional analysis can identify the least congested channels.

DFS utilization: Consider using DFS channels in 5 GHz for additional spectrum, but be aware of radar detection requirements.

Building Material Impacts

Japanese buildings use materials that affect WiFi:

Concrete construction: Concrete blocks WiFi signals effectively. Offices with concrete walls need more APs for adequate coverage.

Metal structures: Elevator shafts, HVAC systems, and metal studs reflect or block signals. Map these obstacles when planning coverage.

Glass considerations: Low-E glass can reflect WiFi signals. Test coverage near extensive glass walls.

Traditional materials: Older buildings with traditional Japanese materials may have different RF characteristics than modern buildings.

Regulatory Compliance

Japan has specific WiFi regulations:

Certified equipment: Only use WiFi equipment certified for use in Japan. Uncertified equipment is illegal and may cause interference.

Power limits: Japan has specific power limits. Ensure APs are configured to comply.

DFS requirements: Some channels require DFS. If using these channels, ensure proper configuration.

Vendor Support in Japan

Consider vendor support availability:

Local support: Choose vendors with Japan-based support. Overseas support may not understand Japan-specific issues.

Documentation: Ensure documentation is available in Japanese if needed by your team.

Warranty service: Verify warranty service is available in Japan for hardware replacement.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing WiFi assessment and troubleshooting services for international companies operating in Japan. Our certified wireless engineers diagnose and resolve WiFi problems using professional tools including Ekahau. From quick troubleshooting to comprehensive redesign, we help you achieve reliable WiFi performance in Tokyo's challenging RF environment. Contact us to schedule a WiFi assessment.

---

Related Articles

  • Ekahau Wireless Survey Japan: Professional WiFi Assessment and Design
  • Enterprise WiFi Japan: Design Standards for High-Performance Networks
  • Wireless Site Survey Tokyo: When, Why, and How to Assess Your WiFi

title: Enterprise WiFi Japan: Design Standards for High-Performance Networks
excerpt: Design enterprise WiFi networks for Tokyo offices. Learn about WiFi 6, capacity planning, security standards, and best practices for high-density environments in Japan.
htmlContent:
Enterprise WiFi Japan: Design Standards for High-Performance Networks

When a global consulting firm redesigned their Tokyo office WiFi in 2025, they approached it like their offices in London and New York. They specified the same access point model, the same density (one AP per 150 square meters), and the same configuration templates. The result was disappointing. In the open office area, where 80 consultants worked in close proximity, WiFi speeds dropped to a crawl during peak hours. Video conferencing was unusable. The conference rooms, designed with floor-to-ceiling glass walls for aesthetics, had dead zones where signals couldn't penetrate. And the guest network, using the same infrastructure as the corporate network, created security concerns. After six months of complaints, they brought in wireless specialists to redesign the network. The new design used WiFi 6 access points with higher density (one AP per 75 square meters), separate RF profiles for different areas, dedicated spectrum for video conferencing, and network segmentation that isolated guest traffic. The cost of the redesign was ¥1.2 million—but the productivity gains and user satisfaction improvements were immediate and sustained.

This story illustrates why enterprise WiFi design matters, and why Tokyo's unique environment requires specific approaches. This guide explains the standards and best practices for designing high-performance enterprise WiFi networks in Japan.

Enterprise WiFi Requirements vs. Consumer WiFi

Enterprise WiFi has requirements that consumer-grade solutions can't meet.

Reliability and Uptime Expectations

Enterprise WiFi must be reliable:

Availability targets: Enterprise networks typically target 99.9% availability or higher. This means less than 9 hours of downtime per year.

Redundancy: Enterprise designs include redundancy so that single points of failure don't take down the network. This may include redundant APs, controllers, and internet connections.

Proactive monitoring: Enterprise networks are monitored continuously with alerts for problems. Issues are addressed before users notice them.

SLA support: Enterprise vendors provide service level agreements with guaranteed response times for problems.

Capacity and Density Requirements

Enterprise networks must support high density:

User density: Enterprise environments may have 50-100+ users per access point in open office areas. Consumer APs are designed for 10-20 users.

Device density: Each user may have 2-3 devices (laptop, phone, tablet). A 100-person office might have 250+ connected devices.

Application requirements: Enterprise users run bandwidth-intensive applications like video conferencing, file sharing, and cloud applications. Consumer networks are designed for web browsing and streaming.

Concurrent usage: In enterprise environments, many users are active simultaneously. Peak usage periods see heavy concurrent demand.

Security and Compliance Standards

Enterprise WiFi must meet security standards:

Authentication: Enterprise networks use strong authentication like 802.1X with RADIUS, not simple passwords.

Encryption: WPA3 encryption is standard for enterprise networks, providing stronger security than WPA2.

Network segmentation: Enterprise networks segment traffic by user type, device type, and application. Guest traffic is isolated from corporate traffic.

Compliance: Enterprise networks may need to meet regulatory compliance requirements like PCI DSS, HIPAA, or Japan's APPI.

Management and Scalability Needs

Enterprise networks must be manageable at scale:

Centralized management: Enterprise networks are managed through centralized controllers or cloud platforms, not individual AP configuration.

Scalability: Enterprise networks can scale from dozens to thousands of APs without redesign.

Zero-touch deployment: New APs can be deployed with minimal configuration, automatically joining the network and downloading settings.

Analytics and reporting: Enterprise platforms provide detailed analytics on network usage, performance, and issues.

WiFi Standards: From 802.11n to WiFi 6E

Understanding WiFi standards helps inform design decisions.

WiFi 4 (802.11n): Legacy Standard

802.11n was a major improvement over previous standards:

Released: 2009

Key features: MIMO (multiple antennas), 40 MHz channels, up to 600 Mbps theoretical speeds

Current status: Legacy standard, still common in older devices but not recommended for new deployments

When to use: Only if you have legacy devices that don't support newer standards

WiFi 5 (802.11ac): Current Mainstream

802.11ac is widely deployed:

Released: 2013

Key features: 5 GHz only, wider channels (up to 160 MHz), up to 3.5 Gbps theoretical speeds, MU-MIMO

Current status: Mainstream standard, adequate for most current deployments

When to use: Good for current deployments, but consider WiFi 6 for new installations

WiFi 6 (802.11ax): Current Best Practice

WiFi 6 is the current best practice for enterprise deployments:

Released: 2019

Key features:

  • OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access): More efficient use of spectrum, better for high-density environments
  • Target Wake Time (TWT): Improves battery life for mobile devices
  • 1024-QAM: Higher data rates
  • BSS Coloring: Better performance in dense environments with neighboring networks
  • Both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz support

Performance: Up to 9.6 Gbps theoretical speeds, but real benefit is improved efficiency in dense environments

Current status: Recommended for all new enterprise deployments

When to use: All new enterprise deployments should use WiFi 6

WiFi 6E: Extended Spectrum

WiFi 6E adds 6 GHz spectrum:

Released: 2020

Key features: Access to 6 GHz band with 1,200 MHz of additional spectrum, less congested than 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz

Current status: Growing adoption, but 6 GHz availability varies by country. Japan has approved 6 GHz for WiFi, but device support is still growing.

When to use: Consider for high-density environments where 5 GHz is congested. Ensure client device support before deploying.

Future: WiFi 7 (802.11be)

WiFi 7 is on the horizon:

Expected: 2024-2025

Key features: 320 MHz channels, 4K-QAM, Multi-Link Operation (simultaneous use of multiple bands)

Current status: Not yet available, but on the roadmap

When to use: Not yet applicable for deployment decisions

Enterprise WiFi Design Principles

Core principles for enterprise WiFi design.

Coverage vs. Capacity Planning

Balance coverage and capacity:

Coverage-focused design: Ensures signal throughout the space. Appropriate for low-density areas like warehouses or outdoor spaces.

Capacity-focused design: Ensures adequate bandwidth for users. Appropriate for high-density areas like open offices and conference rooms.

Hybrid approach: Most enterprise designs use a hybrid approach, with capacity-focused design in high-density areas and coverage-focused design in low-density areas.

Density guidelines:

  • Low density (offices, classrooms): One AP per 150-200 square meters
  • Medium density (open offices): One AP per 100-150 square meters
  • High density (conference rooms, auditoriums): One AP per 50-100 square meters

Cell Size and Channel Planning

Manage interference through cell design:

Cell size: The coverage area of each AP. Smaller cells reduce interference but require more APs.

Cell overlap: Adjacent cells should have 15-20% overlap for smooth roaming.

Channel planning: Adjacent cells should use non-overlapping channels to minimize interference.

Power control: Adjust AP power levels to create appropriately sized cells. Higher power isn't always better.

Band Steering and Client Balancing

Optimize client distribution:

Band steering: Encourage capable devices to use 5 GHz instead of 2.4 GHz. 5 GHz has more channels and less congestion.

Client balancing: Distribute clients evenly across APs. Prevents some APs from being overloaded while others are underutilized.

Load balancing: Some enterprise systems can dynamically balance load by adjusting power levels or steering clients.

Roaming Design

Enable smooth client roaming:

Roaming requirements: Clients should be able to move between APs without dropping connections.

Overlap requirements: 15-20% overlap between adjacent cells enables smooth roaming.

Fast roaming: 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition) enables faster roaming for voice and video applications.

Sticky client prevention: Some clients don't roam well. Enterprise systems can help "push" clients to better APs.

High-Density WiFi Design for Tokyo

Tokyo's density requires specific approaches.

Density Challenges in Tokyo Offices

Tokyo offices are often high-density:

Open office designs: Modern offices often use open designs that pack many users into a small area.

Hot-desking: Hot-desking and hoteling increase density as more people share the same space over time.

Meeting spaces: Conference rooms and meeting spaces can have very high density during meetings.

Device proliferation: Each user may have multiple devices, multiplying the number of connected devices.

WiFi 6 Benefits for High-Density Environments

WiFi 6 is specifically designed for high-density environments:

OFDMA: Divides channels into smaller sub-channels, allowing more efficient use of spectrum. Multiple users can be served simultaneously.

BSS Coloring: Helps APs distinguish between their own traffic and interference from neighboring networks. Improves performance in dense RF environments.

Target Wake Time: Allows devices to schedule when they wake up to communicate, reducing contention and improving battery life.

1024-QAM: Higher data rates enable faster transfers, reducing airtime usage.

AP Density Recommendations for Tokyo

Higher density is typically needed in Tokyo:

Open office areas: One AP per 75-100 square meters (versus 150+ in less dense environments)

Conference rooms: One AP per 50-75 square meters, or dedicated APs for larger rooms

High-density spaces: Auditoriums, cafeterias, and event spaces may need APs every 30-50 square meters

Coverage areas: Lobbies, hallways, and outdoor areas may be lower density

Channel Planning in Congested Environments

Channel planning is critical in Tokyo:

2.4 GHz limitations: Only three non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11). In dense environments, 2.4 GHz is often completely saturated.

5 GHz advantages: 24 non-overlapping channels in 5 GHz. Use 5 GHz as much as possible.

DFS channels: Additional channels available with DFS. Consider using DFS channels if your equipment supports them.

6 GHz future: As WiFi 6E adoption grows, 6 GHz will provide additional spectrum.

Automatic channel selection: Enterprise systems can automatically select optimal channels based on RF environment.

Enterprise WiFi Security Standards

Security is critical for enterprise WiFi.

Authentication Methods

Strong authentication is essential:

WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X): Uses RADIUS server for authentication. Each user has unique credentials. Current standard for enterprise networks.

WPA3-Enterprise: Enhanced security over WPA2. Uses 192-bit encryption for sensitive data environments. Recommended for new deployments.

Certificate-based authentication: Uses client certificates for authentication. More secure than passwords but requires certificate management infrastructure.

Multi-factor authentication: Combines WiFi authentication with additional factors like device certificates or posture assessment.

Network Segmentation

Segment traffic for security:

Corporate SSID: For corporate devices with full network access. Uses strong authentication.

Guest SSID: For visitors and guests. Isolated from corporate network. May use simpler authentication (captive portal).

IoT SSID: For IoT devices with limited access. Restricts what devices can reach.

BYOD SSID: For employee personal devices. May have different access levels than corporate devices.

Role-based access: Within corporate network, use role-based access control to limit what users can access based on their role.

Encryption Standards

Use strong encryption:

WPA2/AES: Minimum acceptable encryption. Uses AES-CCMP cipher.

WPA3: Enhanced encryption with individualized data encryption. Prevents eavesdropping even on the same network.

GCMP-256: Galois/Counter Mode Protocol with 256-bit encryption. Used in WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode.

Monitoring and Threat Detection

Monitor for security threats:

Rogue AP detection: Detect unauthorized access points that may be security threats.

Client isolation: Prevent clients on the same network from communicating with each other.

Intrusion detection: Monitor for suspicious activity and potential attacks.

Compliance monitoring: Ensure network meets compliance requirements (PCI DSS, etc.).

Vendor Selection for Enterprise WiFi

Choose the right vendor for your needs.

Major Enterprise WiFi Vendors

Cisco:

  • Market leader with comprehensive portfolio
  • Catalyst and Meraki product lines
  • Strong enterprise features and integration
  • Premium pricing
  • Excellent for large, complex environments

Aruba (HPE):

  • Strong competitor to Cisco
  • Good wireless performance and features
  • Often more cost-effective than Cisco
  • Strong ClearPass for access control
  • Good for mid-market to enterprise

Juniper Mist:

  • AI-driven wireless platform
  • Strong analytics and automation
  • Good user experience focus
  • Growing enterprise presence
  • Good for organizations wanting modern, cloud-managed WiFi

Ruckus (CommScope):

  • Strong RF performance
  • Good for challenging RF environments
  • Competitive pricing
  • Good for organizations prioritizing coverage and performance

Ubiquiti:

  • Lower-cost option
  • Good for smaller deployments
  • Less enterprise-grade features
  • Good for budget-conscious organizations

Cloud-Managed vs. On-Premises

Choose management model:

Cloud-managed:

  • Simplified management through cloud portal
  • Automatic updates and feature additions
  • Subscription pricing
  • Examples: Cisco Meraki, Aruba Central, Juniper Mist

On-premises:

  • Controllers on-site
  • More control over infrastructure
  • Capital expense model
  • Examples: Cisco Catalyst, Aruba Mobility Controllers

Hybrid: Some vendors offer hybrid options with both cloud and on-premises elements.

Selection Criteria

Evaluate vendors based on:

Features: Does the vendor support the features you need? (WiFi 6, WPA3, etc.)

Performance: How does the vendor perform in your specific environment?

Scalability: Can the solution scale to meet your growth needs?

Integration: Does it integrate with your existing infrastructure?

Support: What level of support is available in Japan?

Cost: Total cost of ownership including hardware, licenses, and support.

Implementation Best Practices

Deploy enterprise WiFi effectively.

Pre-Deployment Planning

Plan before deploying:

Requirements gathering: Document requirements including coverage, capacity, security, and applications.

Site survey: Conduct predictive or active site survey to plan AP placement.

Design validation: Validate design with stakeholders before procurement.

Pilot testing: Consider pilot deployment in a limited area before full rollout.

Phased Deployment

Deploy in phases:

Phase 1: Core areas: Deploy to most critical areas first (main office areas, conference rooms).

Phase 2: Secondary areas: Deploy to secondary areas (lobbies, break rooms, outdoor spaces).

Phase 3: Optimization: Optimize based on feedback and performance data.

Testing and Validation

Test thoroughly:

Coverage testing: Verify coverage throughout the space.

Performance testing: Test performance under load.

Roaming testing: Verify smooth roaming between APs.

Application testing: Test critical applications on the new network.

Security testing: Verify security configuration.

Documentation and Training

Document and train:

Network documentation: Document design, configuration, and procedures.

User documentation: Provide user guides for connecting to WiFi.

Admin training: Train administrators on management tools and procedures.

Support training: Train support staff on troubleshooting.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, specializing in enterprise WiFi design and deployment for international companies operating in Japan. We design high-performance WiFi networks using WiFi 6 technology, optimized for Tokyo's high-density environment. From site surveys to deployment to ongoing management, we provide end-to-end WiFi services that meet enterprise requirements for performance, security, and reliability. Contact us for a free WiFi design consultation.

  • Wireless Site Survey Tokyo: When, Why, and How to Assess Your WiFi

title: Wireless Site Survey Tokyo: When, Why, and How to Assess Your WiFi
excerpt: Learn when to conduct a wireless site survey in Tokyo, what types of surveys are available, what to expect, and how survey results improve WiFi performance.
htmlContent:
Wireless Site Survey Tokyo: When, Why, and How to Assess Your WiFi

A mid-sized law firm in Tokyo's Marunouchi district was preparing to move into a newly renovated office space in 2025. Their IT director, who had managed several office moves before, assumed that placing access points in the ceiling grid at regular intervals would provide adequate WiFi coverage. He ordered eight access points for their 400-square-meter floor plate and scheduled installation for the week before move-in. During the first week of occupancy, the problems became apparent. The corner offices, with their floor-to-ceiling glass walls, had weak signals despite being close to APs. The large conference room, designed to host 20 people, couldn't support more than 10 concurrent video calls without severe degradation. And the filing room, with its metal cabinets, was a complete dead zone. The IT director realized his mistake: he had deployed WiFi based on assumptions rather than data. A professional wireless site survey conducted after the fact revealed that he needed 12 APs, not 8, with different placement and power settings. The cost of the survey and reconfiguration was ¥600,000. The cost of his assumption-based approach: weeks of user complaints, lost productivity, and the embarrassment of having to explain to partners why their new office had worse WiFi than the old one.

This story illustrates why wireless site surveys matter. WiFi is invisible and unpredictable—what works in one space may fail in another due to building materials, interference, usage patterns, and dozens of other factors. A site survey replaces assumptions with data, ensuring that your WiFi investment delivers the performance your organization needs. This guide explains when to conduct a site survey, what types are available, and how to use survey results effectively.

When Do You Need a Wireless Site Survey?

Not every WiFi situation requires a professional survey, but many benefit from one.

New Construction or Major Renovation

Site surveys are essential for new spaces:

Before construction: A predictive survey during the design phase ensures that network infrastructure is planned correctly. This is the ideal time for a survey—you can specify cabling, power, and mounting locations before walls are closed.

During construction: As construction progresses, conditions may change. A validation survey can confirm that the design remains appropriate or identify needed adjustments.

Before occupancy: A final active survey before employees move in validates that the installed network meets requirements. This is your last chance to fix problems before they affect users.

For new construction, the cost of a survey (typically ¥150,000-300,000 for a medium-sized office) is small compared to the cost of fixing problems after occupancy. It's much easier to add an access point during construction than after the ceiling is closed.

Existing WiFi Problems

If you're experiencing WiFi problems, a survey can identify root causes:

Coverage issues: Users report dead zones where WiFi doesn't work. A survey maps actual coverage and identifies gaps.

Performance problems: WiFi is slow or unreliable even where signal appears strong. A survey can identify interference, channel overlap, capacity limitations, or configuration issues.

User complaints: Persistent user complaints about WiFi suggest underlying problems that a survey can diagnose.

Application failures: Video conferencing, cloud applications, or other network-dependent applications don't work reliably.

A survey transforms vague complaints like "the WiFi is bad" into specific, actionable findings like "the conference room has a 15 dB coverage gap due to the metal whiteboard, which can be resolved by adding an AP in the ceiling tile at coordinates X,Y."

Pre-Purchase Validation

Before investing in WiFi equipment, a survey can validate your plans:

Vendor evaluation: Different vendors' equipment performs differently. A survey can compare options in your actual environment.

Quantity validation: Vendors may recommend a certain number of access points, but is that right for your space? A survey can validate or adjust the recommendation.

Budget justification: A survey provides data to justify WiFi investment. If the survey shows problems, it helps build the case for funding.

Technology selection: Should you use WiFi 5 or WiFi 6? Do you need mesh or traditional APs? A survey can inform these decisions.

Compliance and Audit Requirements

Some organizations require surveys for compliance:

Regulatory compliance: Some regulations require documented WiFi coverage for safety or operational reasons.

Insurance requirements: Some cyber insurance policies require documented network assessments.

Audit preparation: For organizations subject to IT audits, a survey demonstrates due diligence in network design.

Due diligence: In M&A transactions, a WiFi survey may be part of IT due diligence.

Major Changes to Existing Networks

Significant changes warrant a survey:

Adding capacity: If you're adding many users or devices, a survey can determine if your existing infrastructure can handle the load.

Changing usage patterns: If you're implementing video conferencing, moving to cloud applications, or making other changes that affect network usage, a survey can assess whether your WiFi can support the new requirements.

Expanding to new areas: If you're expanding into adjacent space or adding floors, a survey ensures the new areas are properly covered.

Upgrading equipment: If you're upgrading from older WiFi standards to WiFi 6, a survey can optimize the new deployment.

Types of Wireless Site Surveys

Different survey types serve different purposes.

Predictive Site Surveys

Predictive surveys are conducted using software before any equipment is installed:

How they work: The surveyor creates a digital model of your space in software like Ekahau, specifying wall materials, floor plans, and expected usage. The software simulates how WiFi signals will propagate and predicts coverage, performance, and interference.

What you get: Predictive reports showing expected coverage heatmaps, recommended AP placement, channel plans, and performance predictions.

Advantages:

  • Can be done before construction or equipment purchase
  • Faster and less expensive than physical surveys
  • Allows "what-if" analysis of different designs
  • No disruption to existing operations

Limitations:

  • Accuracy depends on quality of input data
  • Can't account for all real-world factors
  • Predictions are estimates, not measurements
  • May need validation with physical survey

Best for: New construction, major renovations, budget planning, initial design

Cost: ¥100,000-250,000 depending on space size and complexity

Passive Site Surveys

Passive surveys measure the RF environment without connecting to a network:

How they work: The surveyor walks through the space with a laptop or tablet running survey software that listens to all WiFi signals in the environment. The software maps signal strength from all access points and analyzes the RF environment.

What you get: Heatmaps showing signal strength, channel utilization, interference sources, and coverage analysis.

Advantages:

  • Shows actual RF environment
  • Identifies interference sources
  • Doesn't require a functioning network
  • Can be done before your network is installed

Limitations:

  • Doesn't measure actual throughput or performance
  • Only shows signal strength, not network quality
  • Requires physical presence in the space

Best for: Understanding RF environment, planning new networks, identifying interference

Cost: ¥150,000-350,000 depending on space size

Active Site Surveys

Active surveys measure actual network performance:

How they work: The surveyor walks through the space while connected to the WiFi network, running tests that measure throughput, latency, and other performance metrics. The software maps actual performance throughout the space.

What you get: Heatmaps showing actual throughput, latency, and performance metrics. This shows what users actually experience.

Advantages:

  • Measures actual performance, not just signal strength
  • Shows what users experience
  • Validates that the network meets requirements
  • Identifies performance problems

Limitations:

  • Requires a functioning network to test
  • Only measures your network, not interference from others
  • Requires physical presence

Best for: Validating existing networks, troubleshooting performance problems, post-installation validation

Cost: ¥150,000-350,000 depending on space size and testing requirements

Hybrid Approaches

Many projects benefit from multiple survey types:

Predictive + validation: Start with predictive survey for design, then validate with active survey after installation.

Passive + active: Combine passive survey to understand RF environment with active survey to measure performance.

Initial + ongoing: Conduct comprehensive survey initially, then periodic smaller surveys to validate ongoing performance.

The Site Survey Process: What to Expect

Understanding the process helps you prepare and evaluate results.

Pre-Survey Planning

Before the survey, the surveyor gathers information:

Floor plans: Accurate floor plans are essential. CAD files are preferred, but PDFs or even hand-drawn plans can work if dimensions are accurate. The surveyor needs to know wall locations, materials, and dimensions.

Current infrastructure: If there's existing WiFi, the surveyor needs to know what's installed—access point models, locations, and configurations. This includes both your network and neighboring networks if known.

Usage requirements: How many users? What applications? What performance is needed? A guest network has different requirements than a network supporting 4K video conferencing.

Constraints: Are there areas where APs can't be installed? Aesthetic requirements? Budget limitations? Any known problems or concerns?

Access arrangements: The surveyor will need access to all areas that need coverage, including conference rooms, offices, and common areas. Arrange access in advance.

On-Site Survey Execution

The actual survey involves walking the space:

Duration: A typical survey of a medium-sized office (500-1000 square meters) takes 2-4 hours. Larger or more complex spaces take longer.

Process: The surveyor walks through the space systematically, following a grid pattern to ensure complete coverage. They may stop at various points to take measurements or run tests.

Disruption: Passive and predictive surveys cause no disruption. Active surveys may generate some traffic on the network but typically don't affect users.

What you need to provide:

  • Access to all areas
  • Information about current infrastructure
  • Someone to answer questions that arise
  • For active surveys: network credentials to connect

Data Analysis and Reporting

After the survey, the surveyor analyzes data and produces reports:

Timeline: Report delivery typically takes 3-5 business days after the survey. Complex surveys may take longer.

Report contents: A comprehensive report typically includes:

  • Executive summary of findings
  • Coverage heatmaps (signal strength, SNR, throughput)
  • Problem identification and analysis
  • Specific recommendations
  • Bill of materials (if new equipment is recommended)
  • Implementation guidance

Presentation: Many surveyors will walk through findings with you to ensure understanding and answer questions.

Implementation Support

Many surveyors can help implement recommendations:

Design refinement: Based on findings, the surveyor may refine the design or provide alternative options.

Equipment specification: The surveyor can specify exact equipment models and quantities needed.

Implementation: Some surveyors also provide implementation services. Others may partner with installers or can recommend qualified contractors.

Validation: After implementation, a follow-up survey can validate that problems are resolved.

Understanding Survey Results and Heatmaps

Survey reports include visual heatmaps that make WiFi performance visible.

Signal Strength Heatmaps

Signal strength maps show WiFi coverage:

What they show: WiFi signal strength (RSSI) throughout the surveyed space. Typically shown in colors: green/blue for strong signals, yellow for moderate, red for weak.

How to read: Look for consistent coverage throughout the space. Dead zones show as red or white areas where signal is inadequate.

What to look for:

  • Coverage gaps that need additional APs
  • Areas of weak signal that may need AP relocation or power adjustment
  • Consistent coverage in critical areas

Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Heatmaps

SNR maps show signal quality:

What they show: The ratio of WiFi signal strength to background noise. Higher SNR means better signal quality.

How to read: SNR above 25 dB is good; 15-25 dB is acceptable; below 15 dB is poor.

What to look for: Areas with good signal strength but poor SNR indicate interference problems. These areas may need channel changes or interference mitigation.

Throughput Prediction Heatmaps

Throughput maps estimate performance:

What they show: Estimated data rates throughout the space based on signal strength, interference, and device capabilities.

How to read: Higher throughput (shown in green/blue) is better. Look for areas where throughput drops below requirements.

What to look for: Areas where predicted throughput doesn't meet application requirements. These may need additional capacity or interference mitigation.

Channel Overlap Maps

Channel overlap maps show interference:

What they show: Where access points on the same or overlapping channels interfere with each other.

How to read: Overlap is shown where AP coverage areas intersect on the same channel. This causes interference.

What to look for: Excessive overlap that indicates poor channel planning. Recommendations should include channel reassignments to minimize overlap.

Secondary Coverage Maps

Secondary coverage shows redundancy:

What they show: Whether there's adequate backup coverage if an access point fails.

How to read: Areas with secondary coverage can be served by multiple APs. Areas without secondary coverage depend on a single AP.

What to look for: Critical areas should have secondary coverage for redundancy. If a single AP failure would create a dead zone in an important area, consider adding coverage.

Tokyo-Specific Survey Considerations

Tokyo's environment creates specific survey requirements.

High-Density RF Environment

Tokyo's density affects surveys:

Neighboring networks: In a typical Tokyo office building, you'll see 20-50 neighboring WiFi networks. This creates a crowded RF environment that surveys must account for.

Interference analysis: Surveys should include analysis of neighboring networks and their impact on your design. Channel planning is critical.

Spectrum utilization: The 2.4 GHz band is often completely saturated in Tokyo. Surveys should emphasize 5 GHz and consider DFS channels.

Building Material Variations

Japanese buildings use diverse materials:

Concrete construction: Many Tokyo buildings use concrete extensively. Concrete blocks WiFi signals, requiring more APs for coverage.

Traditional materials: Older buildings may use traditional Japanese materials with different RF characteristics.

Modern materials: Glass, metal, and composite materials affect WiFi differently. Surveys must account for actual building materials.

Mixed construction: Buildings with mixed construction types require careful analysis of how signals propagate through different materials.

Multi-Tenant Building Challenges

Most Tokyo offices are in multi-tenant buildings:

Shared infrastructure: Some buildings provide shared internet or structured cabling. Surveys should account for what's provided versus what you need to add.

Building restrictions: Buildings may have restrictions on AP placement, cabling routes, or equipment installation. Surveyors should understand these constraints.

Coordination with building management: Surveys may need building management approval for access to common areas, risers, or equipment rooms.

Regulatory Compliance

Japan has specific WiFi regulations:

Certified equipment: Surveys should specify equipment certified for use in Japan. Uncertified equipment is illegal.

Power limits: Japan has specific power limits for WiFi. Survey recommendations must comply with these limits.

DFS requirements: Some channels require Dynamic Frequency Selection. Surveys should indicate which channels are recommended and any DFS considerations.

DIY vs. Professional Site Surveys

Should you conduct your own survey or hire a professional?

When DIY Is Sufficient

DIY surveys may be sufficient for:

Small spaces: A small office with simple requirements may not need professional analysis.

Basic validation: If you've installed equipment and just want to validate that it works, free tools may suffice.

Budget constraints: If budget is extremely limited, DIY is better than no survey.

Simple environments: Spaces without challenging RF environments or complex requirements.

When to Hire a Professional

Professional surveys are warranted for:

Business-critical WiFi: If WiFi is essential to business operations, professional design is justified.

Large or complex spaces: Offices over 500 square meters, multi-floor deployments, or spaces with complex layouts benefit from professional analysis.

High-density environments: Dense environments require careful capacity planning that benefits from professional expertise.

Performance problems: If you're experiencing WiFi problems you can't solve, professional analysis can identify root causes.

New construction: For new construction, professional design during planning prevents costly problems later.

Cost-Benefit Analysis

Consider the costs and benefits:

Professional survey cost: ¥150,000-500,000 depending on space size and complexity.

Cost of WiFi problems: Lost productivity, support tickets, user frustration, and potential business impact. For a 50-person office, this can easily exceed ¥1 million annually.

Cost of wrong design: Implementing the wrong solution wastes money and may make problems worse. Adding APs without proper analysis can increase interference.

Value of right design: Professional survey identifies the right solution the first time, saving time and money.

For most business environments, professional surveys are cost-effective insurance against WiFi problems.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing professional wireless site surveys using Ekahau for international companies operating in Japan. Our certified survey engineers conduct predictive, passive, and active surveys to design, troubleshoot, and optimize WiFi networks in Tokyo's challenging RF environment. From small offices to large enterprise deployments, we provide the data-driven analysis you need for reliable WiFi performance. Contact us to schedule your wireless site survey.

  • Enterprise WiFi Japan: Design Standards for High-Performance Networks
