title: English Version: ITAD Japan: Complete Guide to Secure IT Asset Disposition (2026)
excerpt: English version: Complete guide to IT Asset Disposition in Japan. Learn about APPI compliance, kobutsusho kyoka requirements, NIST 800-88 data destruction, and 10 criteria for choosing an ITAD provider.
htmlContent:
This page is an auto-localized English version generated from the original Japanese article.

ITAD Japan: Complete Guide to Secure IT Asset Disposition (2026)

When a multinational financial services firm in Tokyo retired 200 laptops after a hardware refresh in 2025, they thought they had handled disposal properly. They gave the old machines to an electronics recycler who promised environmentally responsible processing. Six months later, they discovered that sensitive customer data had been recovered from several of those laptops and was being sold on the dark web. The recycler had simply wiped the drives with basic formatting and resold the machines without proper data destruction.

The cost to the company was staggering: regulatory fines under Japan's APPI, legal liability, customer notification costs, and irreparable reputational damage. All because they didn't understand what proper IT Asset Disposition requires in Japan.

ITAD—IT Asset Disposition—is the process of securely and responsibly disposing of obsolete or unwanted IT equipment. In Japan, this process is governed by multiple overlapping regulations including data protection laws, environmental regulations, and commercial licensing requirements. Getting it wrong exposes your organization to data breaches, regulatory penalties, and legal liability. Getting it right requires understanding the full scope of requirements and working with qualified providers.

What Is ITAD and Why It Matters in Japan

IT Asset Disposition encompasses the entire lifecycle of retiring IT equipment, from initial inventory and data sanitization through physical disposal and documentation. It's not simply "throwing away old computers." It's a disciplined process that ensures data security, regulatory compliance, environmental responsibility, and proper documentation.

In Japan, ITAD matters more than in many other markets because of the regulatory environment. The Act on Protection of Personal Information (APPI) creates strict obligations for how organizations handle personal data, including data stored on retired equipment. The 2022 amendments strengthened these requirements and increased penalties for non-compliance. Organizations can face fines up to ¥100 million or 1% of annual revenue, plus criminal penalties for willful violations.

Environmental regulations also come into play. Japan's Home Appliance Recycling Law (特定家庭用機器再商品化法) creates specific requirements for disposing of certain electronic equipment. While business IT equipment has different rules than consumer appliances, environmental responsibility is increasingly expected by stakeholders and, in some cases, required by regulation.

The commercial licensing requirement is unique to Japan and often catches foreign companies by surprise. The kobutsusho kyoka (古物商許可), or secondhand goods dealer license, is required for any business that handles used electronics for resale or recycling. Working with an unlicensed provider creates legal liability for your organization.

Beyond compliance, proper ITAD protects your organization's reputation. Data breaches from improperly disposed equipment regularly make headlines and damage brand trust. Customers, partners, and regulators expect that organizations will handle data responsibly throughout its entire lifecycle, including final disposition.

Japanese Regulations Governing ITAD

Understanding the regulatory landscape is essential for compliance. Japan has multiple overlapping requirements that organizations must navigate.

APPI: Data Protection Requirements

The Act on Protection of Personal Information is the primary data protection law in Japan, and it creates specific obligations for data destruction. Under APPI, organizations must take "necessary and appropriate measures" to protect personal data, including preventing leakage, loss, or damage. This obligation extends to data stored on equipment being retired.

The 2022 amendments to APPI introduced mandatory breach notification requirements. If personal data is leaked due to improper disposal, organizations must report to the Personal Information Protection Commission and may need to notify affected individuals. The notification timeline is tight, often just days, which means organizations need to know immediately if a disposal-related breach occurs.

APPI requires that data destruction methods be appropriate to the sensitivity of the data and the risk of unauthorized access. Simply deleting files or formatting drives is not sufficient for most business data. The law expects organizations to use methods that make data recovery technically infeasible.

Documentation is also required under APPI. Organizations must be able to demonstrate that data was properly destroyed, including records of what equipment was processed, what methods were used, and when destruction occurred. This documentation is essential for regulatory compliance and for defending against claims of improper handling.

Kobutsusho Kyoka: Secondhand Goods Dealer License

The kobutsusho kyoka (古物商許可) is a license required under the Secondhand Articles Dealer Law (古物営業法) for any business that deals in used goods, including electronics. This requirement surprises many foreign companies who assume that recycling or disposal doesn't require special licensing.

Any organization that handles IT equipment for the purpose of resale, recycling, or parts recovery needs this license. This includes not just companies that resell used equipment, but also those that dismantle equipment for materials recovery. The license is issued by the prefectural public safety commission and requires background checks, proper business registration, and compliance with record-keeping requirements.

Working with an unlicensed provider creates several problems. First, it's illegal—the unlicensed provider is operating outside the law, and your organization may be seen as facilitating that illegal activity. Second, unlicensed providers typically don't follow proper data destruction procedures, creating breach risk. Third, if something goes wrong, you have no recourse against an illegal operator. Fourth, regulatory scrutiny of your disposal practices may reveal the unlicensed relationship, creating compliance issues.

To verify that a provider has a kobutsusho kyoka, ask for their license number and verify it with the issuing prefectural public safety commission. The license should specify the types of goods the dealer is authorized to handle. For IT equipment, you want to see authorization for electronics or office equipment.

Home Appliance Recycling Law

While Japan's Home Appliance Recycling Law primarily targets consumer appliances like televisions, refrigerators, and air conditioners, businesses should be aware of environmental regulations affecting electronics disposal. For business IT equipment, the law doesn't mandate specific recycling procedures, but environmental responsibility is increasingly expected.

Many organizations have corporate sustainability policies that require environmentally responsible disposal. This typically means working with recyclers who are certified to environmental standards like R2 (Responsible Recycling) or e-Stewards, which ensure that equipment is processed responsibly and that hazardous materials are handled properly.

Environmental documentation may be required for corporate sustainability reporting or to demonstrate compliance with environmental management systems like ISO 14001. Proper ITAD providers can provide documentation of environmental processing, including certificates of recycling and records of materials recovery.

The ITAD Process: Step-by-Step

Proper ITAD follows a disciplined process that ensures nothing falls through the cracks. Here's how a professional ITAD engagement should work.

Step 1: Asset Inventory and Classification

The process begins with a comprehensive inventory of equipment to be disposed. This inventory should capture asset tags, serial numbers, equipment types, and configurations. The inventory serves multiple purposes: it creates a record for compliance documentation, helps determine appropriate disposal methods, and identifies any equipment that might have residual value.

Classification is important because different types of equipment may require different handling. Servers with multiple hard drives need more complex data destruction than single-drive laptops. Equipment containing particularly sensitive data may require enhanced destruction methods. And equipment with residual value may be candidates for refurbishment and resale rather than recycling.

The inventory should also identify any equipment that contains proprietary or highly sensitive data. Financial services firms, healthcare organizations, and companies with trade secrets may need enhanced handling procedures for certain equipment. This classification should happen before any data destruction begins.

Step 2: Data Sanitization and Destruction

Data destruction is the most critical step in the ITAD process. The method used must be appropriate to the sensitivity of the data and must be verifiable. There are three primary approaches to data destruction, each with different use cases.

Software-based data wiping overwrites storage media with patterns that make original data unrecoverable. This approach is appropriate for most business equipment and allows the media to be reused or resold. However, it requires time—wiping a large hard drive can take hours—and must be verified to ensure completeness. Software wiping is not effective for damaged media or for certain types of solid-state storage.

Cryptographic erasure destroys the encryption keys used to secure data, rendering the data inaccessible without actually overwriting it. This approach is very fast and is appropriate for encrypted storage. However, it requires that the encryption was properly implemented and that all copies of the keys are destroyed. If there's any doubt about the encryption implementation, physical destruction is safer.

Physical destruction involves physically damaging the storage media so that data cannot be recovered. This includes shredding, crushing, degaussing (for magnetic media), and incineration. Physical destruction is the most secure method and is appropriate for highly sensitive data or damaged media that cannot be wiped. However, it eliminates any residual value from the media and requires proper handling of electronic waste.

The NIST 800-88 standard provides detailed guidance on selecting appropriate destruction methods based on data sensitivity and media type. Professional ITAD providers should follow this standard or equivalent frameworks.

Step 3: Verification and Documentation

After data destruction, the process must be verified and documented. Verification ensures that destruction was actually performed and was successful. Documentation creates the records needed for compliance and audit purposes.

Verification methods vary based on the destruction approach. For software wiping, verification involves reading the media to confirm that overwritten patterns are present and that original data is not recoverable. For cryptographic erasure, verification involves confirming that keys are destroyed and that data cannot be decrypted. For physical destruction, verification involves visual confirmation that media has been properly damaged.

Documentation should include a certificate of destruction that specifies what equipment was processed, what destruction methods were used, when destruction occurred, and who performed it. The certificate should have a unique identifier for tracking and should be signed by the provider. This documentation is essential for regulatory compliance and for defending against claims of improper data handling.

Step 4: Physical Disposal and Recycling

Once data has been securely destroyed, the physical equipment can be disposed of. The approach depends on the equipment's condition, residual value, and environmental considerations.

Equipment with residual value may be refurbished and resold. This is environmentally preferable to recycling and can generate revenue that offsets disposal costs. However, resale requires that data has been properly destroyed and that the equipment is in working condition. The kobutsusho kyoka is required for resale.

Equipment without resale value is typically recycled. Proper recycling involves dismantling equipment to recover valuable materials like metals and properly disposing of hazardous materials like batteries and certain circuit board components. Certified recyclers follow environmental standards that ensure responsible processing.

Some equipment may need to be destroyed rather than recycled. This is appropriate for equipment that contains proprietary technology or that cannot be sanitized. Destruction is typically done through industrial shredding.

Step 5: Final Reporting and Audit Trail

The ITAD process concludes with comprehensive reporting that creates an audit trail. This reporting should include the initial inventory, destruction certificates, recycling documentation, and any resale records. The complete package demonstrates that proper procedures were followed and that data was handled appropriately.

This documentation should be retained according to your organization's record retention policies. For most organizations, retaining ITAD records for 7 years is appropriate, though specific industries may have different requirements. The documentation should be readily accessible in case of regulatory inquiry or audit.

NIST 800-88: The Gold Standard for Data Destruction

NIST Special Publication 800-88, "Guidelines for Media Sanitization," is widely recognized as the authoritative standard for data destruction. While it's a US government publication, it's used globally including in Japan as the basis for data destruction programs.

The Three Methods: Clear, Purge, and Destroy

NIST 800-88 defines three categories of sanitization, each appropriate for different situations.

Clear involves overwriting storage media with non-sensitive data. This is the least secure method and is generally only appropriate for low-sensitivity data or for media that will remain within organizational control. Clear is not appropriate for most ITAD scenarios where equipment leaves organizational control.

Purge involves techniques that make data recovery infeasible using state-of-the-art laboratory techniques. For magnetic media, this includes secure erase commands and degaussing. For solid-state storage, this includes cryptographic erasure and secure erase commands designed for flash storage. Purge is appropriate for most business ITAD scenarios.

Destroy involves physical destruction that makes data recovery impossible. This includes shredding, crushing, incinerating, and disintegrating media. Destroy is appropriate for highly sensitive data, damaged media that cannot be purged, or when the highest level of assurance is required.

Selecting the Right Method

NIST 800-88 provides guidance on selecting appropriate methods based on data confidentiality level and media type. For most business IT equipment containing routine business data, purge methods are appropriate. For equipment containing highly sensitive data like financial records, personal information, or trade secrets, destroy methods may be warranted.

The standard also considers environmental factors. Equipment that will be resold or reused should be purged rather than destroyed to preserve value. Equipment that is damaged or obsolete should be destroyed and recycled.

Professional ITAD providers should be able to explain which NIST 800-88 methods they use and why those methods are appropriate for your specific situation. They should also be able to provide verification that the selected methods were properly applied.

Ten Criteria for Choosing an ITAD Provider in Tokyo

Selecting the right ITAD provider is critical for compliance and security. Use these ten criteria to evaluate potential partners.

1. Kobutsusho Kyoka Verification

Verify that the provider holds a valid kobutsusho kyoka for handling used electronics. Ask for the license number and verify it with the issuing prefectural public safety commission. Working with an unlicensed provider creates legal and compliance risk.

2. NIST 800-88 Compliance

Ensure the provider follows NIST 800-88 or equivalent standards for data destruction. They should be able to explain which methods they use and provide verification of proper application. Ask for sample certificates of destruction to verify they meet your documentation requirements.

3. On-Site vs. Off-Site Destruction Options

Understand where data destruction occurs. On-site destruction at your facility provides the highest assurance but costs more. Off-site destruction at the provider's facility is more economical but requires trust in their chain of custody procedures. Some providers offer both options.

4. Chain of Custody Documentation

The provider should maintain complete chain of custody documentation tracking equipment from pickup through final disposition. This documentation should include timestamps, locations, and responsible parties at each step. Gaps in chain of custody create compliance risk.

5. Certificate of Destruction

The provider must provide a certificate of destruction for each batch of equipment processed. The certificate should include unique identifiers, equipment details, destruction methods, dates, and verification information. This documentation is essential for compliance.

6. Environmental Certifications

If environmental responsibility is important to your organization, look for providers with certifications like R2 or e-Stewards. These certifications ensure that equipment is processed responsibly and that hazardous materials are handled properly.

7. Insurance Coverage

Verify that the provider carries adequate insurance for data breaches, environmental liability, and general business operations. Request certificates of insurance and understand what is covered.

8. References and Track Record

Ask for references from similar organizations and verify the provider's track record. How long have they been in business? Have they had any data breaches or compliance issues? A provider's history is a good predictor of future performance.

9. Reporting and Audit Support

The provider should offer comprehensive reporting that meets your compliance and audit needs. This includes detailed destruction certificates, recycling documentation, and any custom reporting your organization requires.

10. Cost Transparency

Understand the complete cost structure, including pickup fees, per-device destruction charges, recycling fees, and any additional costs. Beware of providers with hidden fees or unclear pricing.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing ITAD services with NIST 800-88 compliant data destruction and kobutsusho kyoka licensing. We provide complete chain of custody documentation, certificates of destruction, and environmentally responsible recycling for international companies operating in Japan. Contact us for a free ITAD assessment and quote.

  • Secure Data Wiping Japan: Methods and Compliance
  • Kobutsusho Kyoka and ITAD: Why Your Provider Needs a License

title: English Version: Kobutsusho Kyoka and ITAD: Why Your Provider Needs a Secondhand Goods License
excerpt: English version: Why ITAD providers in Japan need kobutsusho kyoka (secondhand goods license). Learn the legal basis, verification methods, risks of unlicensed providers, and how licensing enables asset resale.
htmlContent:
This page is an auto-localized English version generated from the original Japanese article.

Kobutsusho Kyoka and ITAD: Why Your Provider Needs a Secondhand Goods License

When a European pharmaceutical company retired 150 laptops from their Tokyo office in 2025, they selected an IT recycling company that offered an attractive price and promised environmentally responsible processing. Six months later, Japanese authorities contacted them with troubling news: the recycling company was operating without a kobutsusho kyoka (古物商許可), or secondhand goods dealer license, and was under investigation for illegal handling of used electronics. As the source of the equipment, the pharmaceutical company faced questions about their due diligence and potential liability for facilitating illegal activity.

This scenario illustrates a critical but often overlooked aspect of IT Asset Disposition in Japan: the legal requirement that any business handling used electronics for resale or recycling must hold a kobutsusho kyoka. Working with an unlicensed provider doesn't just create compliance risk—it can expose your organization to legal liability, data breach risk, and reputational damage.

This guide explains what the kobutsusho kyoka is, why it matters for ITAD, how to verify a provider's license, the risks of working with unlicensed operators, and how proper licensing enables legitimate asset recovery and value return.

What Is Kobutsusho Kyoka (Secondhand Goods Dealer License)?

The kobutsusho kyoka is a license required under Japan's Secondhand Articles Dealer Law (古物営業法, Kobutsu Eigyo Ho) for any business that deals in used goods. While the law was originally enacted in 1949 to prevent the fencing of stolen goods, it applies broadly to any business that purchases, sells, or brokers used items—including electronic equipment.

Legal Basis and Requirements

The Secondhand Articles Dealer Law requires licensing for any business that engages in the "purchase or sale of secondhand articles as a business." This includes:

  • Purchasing used equipment for resale
  • Selling used equipment on behalf of others (consignment)
  • Dismantling used equipment for parts or materials recovery
  • Brokering transactions involving used goods

The law defines "secondhand articles" broadly to include virtually all tangible goods, with specific categories including electronics, office equipment, and computer hardware. There is no exemption for business-to-business transactions or for equipment that will be recycled rather than resold.

To obtain a kobutsusho kyoka, a business must:

  1. Register with the prefectural public safety commission (警察署) where their business is located
  2. Submit an application including business details, owner information, and intended scope of operations
  3. Undergo a background check to ensure the applicant has no criminal history that would disqualify them
  4. Pay the required licensing fee (typically ¥10,000-20,000 depending on the prefecture)
  5. Display the license at their place of business

The license specifies the types of goods the dealer is authorized to handle and the location where operations may be conducted. A license for "office equipment" or "electronics" is required for ITAD operations.

Types of Licenses and Their Scopes

Kobutsusho kyoka are categorized by the types of goods the dealer is authorized to handle. For ITAD providers, the relevant categories include:

General secondhand goods (一般古物): This broad category covers most types of used goods and is appropriate for ITAD providers handling various types of equipment.

Specified secondhand goods (特定古物): This category includes specific types of goods with higher risk profiles. Electronics and computer equipment may fall under this category depending on local regulations.

Secondhand goods broker (古物仲介): This license is for businesses that broker transactions between buyers and sellers without taking ownership of the goods. Some ITAD providers operate under this model.

The scope of the license determines what types of equipment the provider can legally handle. A provider licensed only for "office furniture" cannot legally process computers or servers, even if they claim to offer ITAD services.

Application Process and Requirements

Obtaining a kobutsusho kyoka requires submitting a detailed application to the prefectural public safety commission. The application includes:

  • Business registration documents
  • Identification of the business owner and responsible managers
  • Description of the business premises
  • Statement of the types of goods to be handled
  • Criminal background check for the applicant

The background check is thorough and includes verification that the applicant has not been convicted of crimes related to theft, fraud, or handling stolen goods. This screening is one of the key protections the licensing system provides—ensuring that those who handle used goods are trustworthy.

Once approved, the license must be renewed periodically (typically every 3-5 years depending on the prefecture) and the licensee must maintain records of all transactions for inspection by authorities.

Why ITAD Providers Need Kobutsusho Kyoka

The requirement for kobutsusho kyoka in ITAD operations surprises many foreign companies who assume that recycling or disposal doesn't require special licensing. However, the legal framework in Japan is clear: any business that handles used electronics for the purpose of resale, parts recovery, or materials recycling must be licensed.

Legal Requirement for Handling Used Electronics

When an ITAD provider accepts your retired equipment, they are legally "purchasing" used goods (even if no money changes hands, the transfer of ownership constitutes a purchase under the law). If they then resell that equipment, dismantle it for parts, or process it for materials recovery, they are engaged in the business of dealing in secondhand goods.

This means that any ITAD provider who:

  • Accepts your used equipment for resale
  • Dismantles equipment for parts recovery
  • Processes equipment for materials recycling
  • Brokers the sale of your equipment to third parties

...must hold a valid kobutsusho kyoka covering the types of equipment they handle.

Operating without this license is a criminal offense. The unlicensed provider can face fines and imprisonment, and your organization may be seen as facilitating their illegal operation.

Consequences of Operating Without License

For an ITAD provider, operating without a kobutsusho kyoka carries significant legal risks:

Criminal penalties: Operating as a secondhand goods dealer without a license is punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment or fines up to ¥1 million.

Business closure: Authorities can order unlicensed operations to cease immediately, potentially leaving your equipment in limbo.

Confiscation of goods: Equipment held by an unlicensed dealer may be confiscated by authorities, creating complications for your asset disposition.

Loss of trust: Discovery of unlicensed operation damages the provider's reputation and raises questions about their legitimacy.

For your organization, engaging an unlicensed provider creates several problems:

Legal risk: While not directly criminal, engaging an unlicensed provider may be seen as facilitating illegal activity. This is particularly concerning for regulated industries.

Compliance risk: If your ITAD practices are audited, the lack of proper licensing for your provider will be flagged as a deficiency.

Data breach risk: Unlicensed providers are less likely to follow proper data destruction procedures, creating risk of data exposure.

No recourse: If something goes wrong with an unlicensed provider, you have limited legal recourse against an illegal operator.

How It Protects Your Business

Working with a licensed provider protects your organization in several ways:

Legal compliance: You can demonstrate that you performed due diligence in selecting a properly licensed provider, protecting you from liability.

Accountability: Licensed providers are regulated and can be held accountable for their actions. Unlicensed operators can simply disappear if problems arise.

Data security: The licensing process includes background checks, providing some assurance that the provider is trustworthy for handling your sensitive equipment.

Audit trail: Licensed providers must maintain transaction records, creating documentation for your compliance needs.

Proper procedures: Licensed providers are more likely to follow proper procedures for data destruction, environmental compliance, and asset handling.

How to Verify Your ITAD Provider's Kobutsusho Kyoka

Before engaging an ITAD provider, you should verify that they hold a valid kobutsusho kyoka. This verification is a basic due diligence step that protects your organization.

Where to Check License Status

Kobutsusho kyoka are issued by prefectural public safety commissions. To verify a license:

  1. Ask the provider for their license number and issuing prefecture
  2. Contact the public safety commission in that prefecture
  3. Provide the license number and business name
  4. Request confirmation that the license is valid and current

Some prefectures offer online verification systems, while others require phone or written inquiries. The process is straightforward and authorities are accustomed to verification requests.

What Information the License Contains

A valid kobutsusho kyoka contains:

  • License number (license number format varies by prefecture)
  • Business name and address
  • Name of the licensee (business owner)
  • Types of goods authorized (e.g., "office equipment," "electronics")
  • Date of issue and expiration
  • Any restrictions or conditions

Review this information to ensure that:

  • The license is current (not expired)
  • The business name matches the provider you're engaging
  • The authorized goods include the types of equipment you need to dispose of
  • There are no restrictions that would prevent them from handling your equipment

Red Flags Indicating Unlicensed Operation

Watch for these warning signs that a provider may be operating without proper licensing:

Refusal to provide license information: A legitimate provider should readily share their license number. Hesitation or refusal is a major red flag.

Significantly lower prices: Unlicensed operators often undercut licensed providers on price because they avoid licensing costs and regulatory compliance. If a quote seems too good to be true, it probably is.

No physical business address: Licensed providers must have a registered business location. Be wary of providers who only offer mobile phone contact or refuse to disclose their address.

Cash-only transactions: Licensed providers typically offer standard business payment terms (invoicing, bank transfer). Demanding cash payment may indicate an attempt to avoid documentation.

No written contracts: Licensed providers will offer written contracts that specify services, pricing, and terms. Reluctance to put agreements in writing is suspicious.

Vague answers about licensing: If a provider says they "don't need" a license or that their "business model is different," this is a warning sign. The requirements are clear, and legitimate providers know they need to be licensed.

Questions to Ask Your Provider

During your evaluation process, ask these specific questions:

"What is your kobutsusho kyoka license number and which prefecture issued it?"

"What types of equipment are you authorized to handle under your license?"

"Can you provide a copy of your current license for our records?"

"Are there any restrictions on your license that would affect our engagement?"

A legitimate provider will answer these questions clearly and provide documentation. Evasive answers or claims that licensing "doesn't apply" should disqualify the provider.

Documentation You Should Receive

For your compliance records, you should receive and retain:

  • Copy of the provider's current kobutsusho kyoka
  • Written contract specifying services and terms
  • Certificates of destruction for equipment processed
  • Chain of custody documentation
  • Environmental compliance documentation (if applicable)

This documentation demonstrates that you performed due diligence in selecting a licensed provider and that proper procedures were followed.

The Risks of Using an Unlicensed ITAD Provider

The risks of engaging an unlicensed ITAD provider extend beyond compliance concerns to include data security, legal liability, and operational problems.

Legal Liability for Your Business

While operating without a kobutsusho kyoka is primarily the provider's offense, your organization may face questions about why you engaged an unlicensed operator. In regulated industries, this could trigger regulatory scrutiny or enforcement action.

If the unlicensed provider is discovered and shut down by authorities, your equipment may be caught up in the investigation. You may need to provide documentation about what equipment was transferred and when, creating administrative burden and potential compliance issues.

In extreme cases, if the unlicensed provider is found to be handling stolen goods or engaging in other criminal activity, your organization could face accusations of facilitating their operation. While this is rare, it's a risk that proper due diligence can avoid.

Data Security Risks

Unlicensed providers are less likely to follow proper data destruction procedures. They may:

  • Skip data wiping entirely and simply resell equipment as-is
  • Use inadequate wiping methods that leave data recoverable
  • Fail to maintain chain of custody documentation
  • Lack proper security controls for handling sensitive equipment

The result is increased risk of data breach. If customer data, employee information, or proprietary data is recovered from equipment you disposed of, your organization faces regulatory penalties, legal liability, and reputational damage.

No Recourse for Problems

If something goes wrong with an unlicensed provider—equipment isn't processed as promised, data is breached, or environmental regulations are violated—you have limited recourse. An illegal operator can simply disappear, change their business name, or refuse to cooperate.

Licensed providers, by contrast, are regulated and can be held accountable. They have a business reputation to protect and regulatory obligations to meet. If problems arise, you have avenues for resolution.

Potential Criminal Implications

In extreme cases, knowingly engaging an unlicensed provider could potentially expose your organization to criminal charges for facilitating illegal activity. While prosecution of equipment owners is rare, it's not impossible—particularly if the unlicensed provider is found to be engaged in serious criminal activity.

The safer course is to verify licensing before engaging any ITAD provider and to document that verification for your compliance records.

Reputational Damage

Discovery that you used an unlicensed ITAD provider can damage your organization's reputation. Customers, partners, and regulators expect that you will perform proper due diligence on vendors, especially those handling sensitive data. Failure to do so raises questions about your overall compliance posture.

In an era of increasing focus on supply chain compliance and vendor management, using unlicensed providers is a red flag that can have broader reputational consequences.

Kobutsusho Kyoka and IT Asset Resale

Proper licensing doesn't just ensure compliance—it also enables legitimate asset recovery and value return from retired equipment.

How License Enables Value Recovery

When your ITAD provider has a kobutsusho kyoka, they can legally purchase your used equipment, refurbish it if needed, and resell it to secondary markets. This creates an opportunity for value recovery that wouldn't be possible with an unlicensed operator.

The typical value recovery process works as follows:

  1. Your provider accepts retired equipment and performs data destruction
  2. Equipment is assessed for resale potential
  3. Working equipment is refurbished (cleaned, tested, repaired if needed)
  4. Refurbished equipment is sold through wholesale or retail channels
  5. You receive a share of the resale proceeds (depending on your contract)

Without proper licensing, this process is illegal. The provider cannot legally take ownership of your equipment for resale, and any "value recovery" they offer may be a sham or may involve illegal transfers.

Legal Requirements for Reselling Used IT Equipment

Reselling used IT equipment in Japan requires:

  • Kobutsusho kyoka covering the types of equipment being sold
  • Proper documentation of the transaction (seller, buyer, equipment details)
  • Compliance with consumer protection laws if selling to end users
  • Proper handling of any personal data remaining on equipment

Licensed ITAD providers understand these requirements and have procedures to ensure compliance. They maintain transaction records, provide appropriate documentation, and follow proper procedures for data destruction before resale.

Documentation for Resale Transactions

When equipment is resold, proper documentation should include:

  • Transfer of ownership records
  • Data destruction certificates
  • Equipment specifications and condition
  • Sale price and buyer information
  • Any warranties or guarantees provided

This documentation protects both you and the buyer, demonstrating that the equipment was properly processed and that ownership was legally transferred.

Tax Implications

Value recovery from asset resale may have tax implications. The proceeds from equipment sales may be considered taxable income, and proper documentation is needed for tax reporting.

Your ITAD provider should provide documentation of any value recovery that includes the information needed for tax compliance. This typically includes:

  • Equipment details (make, model, serial number)
  • Sale price
  • Date of sale
  • Buyer information
  • Any fees deducted

Consult with your tax advisor to understand the specific implications for your organization.

International Resale Considerations

Some ITAD providers export used equipment to international markets. This creates additional considerations:

  • Export regulations and licensing requirements
  • Import regulations in destination countries
  • Data protection requirements in destination jurisdictions
  • Environmental regulations for cross-border waste shipments

Licensed providers should understand these requirements and ensure compliance. Be wary of providers who offer to export equipment without clear explanation of how they handle these issues.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing ITAD services with full kobutsusho kyoka licensing. We offer NIST 800-88 compliant data destruction, value recovery from asset resale, and complete documentation for compliance. Our Minato-ku location serves international companies throughout Tokyo with transparent, legally compliant IT asset disposition services. Contact us for a free ITAD consultation.

The kobutsusho kyoka is a license required under Japan's Secondhand Articles Dealer Law (古物営業法, Kobutsu Eigyo Ho) for any business that deals in used goods. Legal Basis and Requirements

  • Dismantling used equipment for parts or materials recovery
  • Brokering transactions involving used goods

The law defines "secondhand articles" broadly to include virtually all tangible goods, with specific categories including electronics, office equipment, and computer hardware. There is no exemption for business-to-business transactions or for equipment that will be recycled rather than resold.

title: English Version: Data Destruction Certificate Japan: What It Must Include for Compliance
excerpt: English version: Data destruction certificates are essential for APPI compliance in Japan. Learn the 10 required elements, difference from haiki shomeisho, digital vs physical certificates, and red flags for invalid documentation.
htmlContent:
This page is an auto-localized English version generated from the original Japanese article.

Data Destruction Certificate Japan: What It Must Include for Compliance

When Japanese regulators audited a major retailer's data protection practices in 2025, they asked for documentation showing how the company handled data on retired IT equipment. The retailer provided certificates from their ITAD provider—but the certificates lacked critical details like serial numbers, destruction methods, and verification information. The regulators were not satisfied. They couldn't confirm that specific equipment had been properly processed, and they couldn't verify that the destruction methods met APPI requirements. The retailer faced additional scrutiny, regulatory recommendations, and the cost of engaging a new provider to re-document their ITAD practices.

This case illustrates a critical but often overlooked aspect of IT Asset Disposition: the data destruction certificate is not just a formality—it's a compliance document that must contain specific information to satisfy regulatory requirements. An incomplete or invalid certificate is almost as bad as no certificate at all.

This guide explains why data destruction certificates are essential, what elements they must include, how they differ from other Japanese documentation like haiki shomeisho, how to manage certificates effectively, and red flags that indicate a certificate isn't valid.

Why a Data Destruction Certificate Is Essential

Data destruction certificates serve multiple critical functions for organizations in Japan. Understanding these functions helps explain why proper documentation matters.

Legal Protection Under APPI

The Act on Protection of Personal Information (APPI) requires organizations to take "necessary and appropriate measures" to protect personal data, including preventing leakage when equipment is retired. While APPI doesn't explicitly mandate data destruction certificates, these certificates serve as evidence that your organization took appropriate measures.

If a data breach occurs involving equipment you disposed of, or if regulators question your data handling practices, a proper data destruction certificate demonstrates that you followed recognized procedures. The certificate shows that you engaged a qualified provider, that proper methods were used, and that destruction was verified.

Without this documentation, you have no evidence to defend against claims of improper data handling. Regulators or courts may conclude that you failed to take "necessary and appropriate measures," exposing you to penalties and liability.

Audit and Compliance Requirements

Many organizations face audits that require documentation of data destruction practices. These audits may come from:

  • Regulators conducting compliance examinations
  • Customers or partners conducting vendor assessments
  • Internal audit functions reviewing security practices
  • External auditors evaluating financial or operational controls

In each case, auditors expect to see documentation that demonstrates proper data handling. A data destruction certificate is the primary document that shows equipment was properly processed before leaving organizational control.

Organizations seeking or maintaining certifications like ISO 27001 need documented evidence of data destruction practices. ISO 27001 controls specifically require procedures for secure disposal of media, and certificates provide the evidence that those procedures are followed.

Insurance and Liability Considerations

Cyber insurance policies increasingly require evidence of proper data handling practices. If you file a claim related to a data breach, your insurer may ask for documentation showing how you handle data destruction. Proper certificates support your claim and demonstrate that you followed reasonable security practices.

Conversely, the absence of proper documentation may give insurers grounds to deny claims. If you can't prove that retired equipment was properly processed, insurers may argue that you failed to maintain appropriate security controls.

Proof of Due Diligence

Data destruction certificates demonstrate that your organization performed due diligence in selecting and managing ITAD providers. They show that you:

  • Engaged qualified providers who follow recognized standards
  • Required proper data destruction methods
  • Verified that destruction was performed
  • Maintained records for compliance purposes

This due diligence is important for defending against negligence claims. If data is breached from equipment you disposed of, plaintiffs may argue that you were negligent in selecting or overseeing your ITAD provider. Proper certificates help defend against these claims by showing you took reasonable steps.

When You Absolutely Must Have One

Data destruction certificates are essential in any situation where equipment containing sensitive data leaves organizational control. This includes:

  • Routine hardware refresh cycles
  • Office relocations or closures
  • Equipment returns at end of lease
  • Disposal of failed or obsolete equipment
  • Donation of equipment to charities or schools

Even if equipment is believed to be non-functional, data destruction certificates are important. Non-functional drives can often be repaired or read using specialized equipment, so "it didn't work" is not a defense against data breach claims.

Ten Elements Every Data Destruction Certificate Must Include

A proper data destruction certificate is a formal document that contains specific information. While formats vary by provider, these ten elements should be present in every certificate:

1. Date and Time of Destruction

The certificate must specify exactly when destruction occurred. This should include:

  • Date (year, month, day)
  • Time (hour and minute)
  • Time zone (JST for Japan operations)

The date and time are important for establishing when data ceased to exist, which may be relevant for regulatory inquiries or legal proceedings. If questions arise about data handling, the timestamp shows when the data was destroyed.

2. Method of Destruction Used

The certificate must specify what destruction method was used. This should be specific enough to demonstrate that proper procedures were followed. Examples include:

  • "NIST 800-88 Purge using ATA Secure Erase"
  • "NIST 800-88 Destroy using industrial shredding"
  • "Cryptographic erasure with key destruction"

Vague descriptions like "securely wiped" or "data removed" are insufficient. The method should reference recognized standards (like NIST 800-88) and specific techniques.

3. Device/Asset Identification

The certificate must identify the specific equipment that was processed. This should include:

  • Asset tag numbers (your organization's tracking numbers)
  • Serial numbers (manufacturer serial numbers)
  • Make and model information
  • Quantity of devices

Ideally, the certificate should list each device individually with its unique identifiers. For large batches, a detailed inventory may be attached as an appendix, with the certificate referencing that inventory.

The key is that equipment can be uniquely identified and traced from your records to the destruction certificate.

4. Quantity and Type of Media Destroyed

The certificate should specify what types of storage media were processed and in what quantities. This might include:

  • Hard disk drives (HDDs): X units
  • Solid-state drives (SSDs): X units
  • Magnetic tapes: X units
  • Optical media: X units
  • Mobile devices: X units

This information helps verify that all storage media was addressed and provides context for the destruction effort.

5. Standard Followed

The certificate should reference the standard or guideline that destruction methods followed. This is typically:

  • "NIST SP 800-88 Rev. 1" for most business data destruction
  • "DoD 5220.22-M" if that standard is required by your organization
  • "ISO 27001 controls" for organizations following that framework

Referencing recognized standards demonstrates that proper methods were used and provides a basis for evaluating the adequacy of destruction.

6. Location of Destruction

The certificate should specify where destruction occurred. This might be:

  • Your facility address (for on-site destruction)
  • The provider's facility address (for off-site destruction)
  • A specific destruction facility if different from the provider's main location

The location is important for chain of custody documentation and for verifying that destruction occurred in an appropriate facility.

7. Name and Signature of Technician

The certificate should identify who performed the destruction. This includes:

  • Name of the technician or operator
  • Signature (physical or digital)
  • Date of signature

This creates accountability and provides a point of contact if questions arise about the destruction.

8. Company Information and License Numbers

The certificate should identify the provider who performed the destruction. This includes:

  • Company name and address
  • Contact information
  • Kobutsusho kyoka (secondhand goods dealer license) number
  • Any other relevant certifications (NAID, ISO 27001, etc.)

This information allows verification that the provider was qualified to perform the work and creates accountability.

9. Verification Method Used

The certificate should explain how destruction was verified. This might include:

  • "Verification read performed after overwriting"
  • "Visual confirmation of shredding"
  • "Key destruction log reviewed"
  • "Sample verification performed on X% of devices"

Verification demonstrates that destruction was not just attempted but was confirmed to be successful.

10. Unique Certificate Number for Tracking

Every certificate should have a unique identifier that allows it to be tracked and referenced. This number should be:

  • Unique to that specific destruction event
  • Formatted in a way that allows retrieval of records
  • Referenced in any related documentation

The certificate number is essential for audit trails and for retrieving documentation when needed.

Understanding Haiki Shomeisho vs. Data Destruction Certificate

In Japan, you may encounter two types of documentation related to equipment disposal: the data destruction certificate and the haiki shomeisho (廃棄証明書, or certificate of disposal). Understanding the difference is important for compliance.

Difference from Data Destruction Certificate

A data destruction certificate focuses specifically on data sanitization. It documents that data stored on equipment was properly destroyed according to recognized standards. This certificate is essential for data protection compliance.

A haiki shomeisho is a broader document that certifies equipment was disposed of or recycled. It may include information about data destruction, but its primary purpose is to document the physical disposition of equipment—whether it was recycled, destroyed, or otherwise processed.

When Each Is Required

Data destruction certificates are required when equipment contains data that must be protected. This is essentially all business equipment that has been used, since even seemingly innocuous devices may contain configuration information, cached credentials, or other sensitive data.

Haiki shomeisho may be required for:

  • Environmental compliance documentation
  • Asset retirement records
  • Financial records for asset write-offs
  • Lease return documentation

In many cases, organizations need both documents. The data destruction certificate proves that data was handled properly, while the haiki shomeisho proves that equipment was disposed of appropriately.

Legal Standing in Japanese Courts

Both documents can serve as evidence in legal proceedings, but they prove different things. A data destruction certificate is evidence that data was destroyed, which is relevant for data protection claims. A haiki shomeisho is evidence that equipment was disposed of, which is relevant for property or environmental claims.

For data breach litigation or regulatory proceedings, the data destruction certificate is the more relevant document. It directly addresses whether data was properly handled.

Integration with APPI Compliance Documentation

Both documents should be integrated into your APPI compliance documentation. Your compliance records should show:

  • What equipment was retired
  • When data destruction occurred (data destruction certificate)
  • When equipment was disposed of (haiki shomeisho)
  • That proper procedures were followed in both cases

This complete documentation demonstrates that your organization takes data protection seriously and follows proper procedures throughout the equipment lifecycle.

Digital vs. Physical Certificates: Pros and Cons

Data destruction certificates can be issued in physical (paper) or digital formats. Each has advantages and considerations.

Accessibility and Storage

Digital certificates are easily stored, backed up, and retrieved. They can be saved in document management systems, backed up to cloud storage, and searched electronically. This accessibility is valuable when you need to retrieve certificates for audits or regulatory inquiries.

Physical certificates require physical storage space and are harder to search. However, some organizations prefer physical documents for certain types of records, particularly if they have established paper-based recordkeeping systems.

Many organizations maintain both: digital copies for easy access and physical copies as formal records.

Tamper Resistance

Physical certificates with original signatures are difficult to forge or alter. The physical document serves as evidence that the certificate is authentic.

Digital certificates can be secured through digital signatures, encryption, and audit trails. However, they may be more vulnerable to tampering if proper security controls aren't in place.

Reputable providers use secure methods for digital certificates, including PDF signatures, secure document delivery, and audit trails that track access.

Audit Trail Capabilities

Digital certificates can include embedded metadata that tracks when they were created, modified, and accessed. This creates an audit trail that can be valuable for compliance purposes.

Physical certificates rely on external recordkeeping to track access and handling. This can be less precise than digital audit trails.

Integration with Asset Management Systems

Digital certificates can be integrated with asset management systems, allowing you to link certificates directly to asset records. This integration makes it easy to retrieve certificates when needed and ensures that documentation is complete.

Physical certificates require manual linking to asset records, which is more labor-intensive and error-prone.

Legal Admissibility

Both physical and digital certificates are legally admissible in Japan. The Electronic Signature and Certification Services Act provides a framework for electronic signatures, and properly executed digital certificates are legally valid.

However, some organizations prefer physical certificates for high-value or high-risk transactions, viewing them as more "official." This is largely a matter of organizational preference rather than legal requirement.

How to Store and Manage Destruction Certificates

Proper management of data destruction certificates is essential for compliance. Here are best practices for storage and management.

Retention Period Requirements

Data destruction certificates should be retained according to your organization's record retention policy. For most organizations, this is 7 years, which aligns with:

  • Tax record retention requirements
  • General business record retention
  • Potential statute of limitations for claims

Some industries may have longer retention requirements. Financial services, healthcare, and government contractors may need to retain records for 10 years or longer.

When in doubt, retain certificates longer rather than shorter. The storage cost is minimal compared to the compliance risk of missing documentation.

Organization and Indexing

Certificates should be organized in a way that allows quick retrieval. Common approaches include:

  • Organizing by date of destruction
  • Organizing by asset tag or serial number
  • Organizing by department or location
  • Using a document management system with search capabilities

The key is that you can quickly find the certificate for a specific piece of equipment when needed. If regulators ask about a specific laptop, you should be able to locate the destruction certificate within minutes.

Integration with Compliance Audits

Destruction certificates should be integrated into your compliance audit processes. This includes:

  • Regular reviews to ensure all retired equipment has certificates
  • Verification that certificates contain all required elements
  • Confirmation that providers remain qualified
  • Documentation of any exceptions or issues

Regular audits help ensure that your ITAD program remains compliant and that documentation is complete.

Backup and Disaster Recovery

Like all important business records, destruction certificates should be backed up and included in disaster recovery planning. This includes:

  • Regular backups of digital certificates
  • Off-site storage of physical certificates
  • Recovery procedures in case of system failure
  • Testing of recovery procedures

Losing destruction certificates due to system failure or disaster creates the same compliance gap as never having them.

Access Control

Destruction certificates contain information about your IT assets and data handling practices. Access should be controlled to prevent unauthorized disclosure.

For digital certificates, use access controls that limit who can view, modify, or delete records. For physical certificates, use secure storage with controlled access.

Access logs can also be valuable for audit purposes, showing who has reviewed certificates and when.

Red Flags: When a Certificate Isn't Valid

Not all certificates are created equal. Watch for these red flags that indicate a certificate may not be valid or adequate for compliance purposes.

Missing Required Information

A certificate that lacks the ten essential elements described earlier is incomplete and may not satisfy regulatory requirements. Common deficiencies include:

  • Missing serial numbers or asset tags
  • Vague descriptions of destruction methods
  • No verification information
  • Missing provider license numbers
  • No unique certificate number

If a certificate is missing critical information, request a corrected certificate from the provider. If they can't provide one, this raises questions about their competence and compliance.

Generic Templates Without Specifics

Some providers use generic certificate templates that lack specific information about your equipment. A certificate that says "X laptops securely wiped" without serial numbers, models, or specific methods is insufficient.

Each certificate should be specific to the equipment you disposed of, with unique identifiers that allow traceability.

No Verification Method Documented

A certificate that states destruction was performed but doesn't explain how it was verified is incomplete. Verification is essential for demonstrating that destruction was successful, not just attempted.

If a provider can't explain how they verify destruction, this suggests they may not be following proper procedures.

Unlicensed Provider

If the provider's certificate doesn't include a kobutsusho kyoka number, or if the license number can't be verified, the provider may be unlicensed. Working with unlicensed providers creates compliance risk and may invalidate the certificate's value for regulatory purposes.

Always verify that providers are properly licensed before engaging them, and confirm that license numbers on certificates are valid.

Inability to Trace or Verify

If you can't trace a certificate back to the specific equipment it covers, or if you can't verify the information with the provider, the certificate's value is limited. Proper certificates should be traceable and verifiable.

Test your provider's recordkeeping by occasionally requesting verification of historical certificates. If they can't retrieve records or confirm details, this suggests inadequate documentation practices.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing ITAD services with comprehensive data destruction documentation. Our certificates include all required elements for APPI compliance, with detailed equipment identification, NIST 800-88 method documentation, and complete verification records. We provide both digital and physical certificates with unique tracking numbers for easy retrieval during audits. Contact us for a free consultation on your ITAD documentation needs.

  • NIST 800-88 Japan: Data Sanitization Compliance Guide
