title: English Version: IT Asset Management Japan: Complete Framework for Compliance and Efficiency
excerpt: English version: Build a comprehensive IT asset management program in Japan. Learn about APPI compliance, My Number tracking, lifecycle management, and software selection for Tokyo-based organizations.
htmlContent:
This page is an auto-localized English version generated from the original Japanese article.

IT Asset Management Japan: Complete Framework for Compliance and Efficiency

When a mid-sized pharmaceutical company in Tokyo faced their annual audit in 2025, the IT director thought they were prepared. They had spreadsheets tracking their equipment purchases, maintenance contracts were filed in cabinets, and the finance team had depreciation schedules. But when the auditors asked for a complete inventory of all laptops containing patient data, the IT director couldn't produce it quickly. When they asked how many software licenses were actually in use versus purchased, the answer required days of manual counting. And when regulators asked for documentation showing that retired equipment containing personal information had been properly tracked and destroyed, the records were scattered across multiple systems and departments.

The audit revealed what the IT director had suspected but couldn't quantify: their IT asset management was inadequate for a regulated industry in Japan. They were over-licensed on several software platforms, under-licensed on others, had equipment that was no longer under maintenance but still in production, and lacked the documentation needed to demonstrate APPI compliance for data handling. The remediation project cost ¥8 million and took six months to complete.

This story illustrates why IT Asset Management (ITAM) matters—and why it's particularly challenging in Japan's regulatory environment. This guide provides a comprehensive framework for building an ITAM program that ensures compliance, optimizes costs, and reduces risk.

What Is IT Asset Management and Why It Matters

IT Asset Management is the set of business practices that join financial, contractual, and inventory functions to support life cycle management and strategic decision making for the IT environment. In simpler terms, ITAM is knowing what IT assets you have, where they are, how they're used, and how much they cost—then using that information to make better decisions.

The scope of ITAM includes hardware assets (servers, laptops, networking equipment, mobile devices), software assets (licenses, subscriptions, cloud services), and the relationships between them. A comprehensive ITAM program tracks assets from procurement through deployment, operation, maintenance, and final disposition.

Business Benefits of Proper ITAM

The benefits of effective ITAM extend across the organization:

Cost reduction: Most organizations are over-licensed on software by 20-30%. Proper ITAM identifies unused licenses that can be reclaimed or not renewed, eliminates duplicate purchases, and ensures maintenance contracts cover only equipment still in use. For a 100-person organization, this typically saves ¥3-5 million annually.

Compliance assurance: APPI, My Number regulations, and industry-specific requirements all require knowing what equipment contains sensitive data and ensuring it's properly handled. ITAM provides the tracking and documentation needed to demonstrate compliance.

Risk reduction: Equipment that's no longer under maintenance creates security and operational risk. ITAM identifies these gaps so they can be addressed before they cause problems.

Operational efficiency: When you know what you have and where it is, support is faster, procurement is more strategic, and planning is based on actual data rather than estimates.

Audit readiness: Whether it's a regulatory audit, financial audit, or vendor audit, proper ITAM means you have the documentation needed to respond quickly and confidently.

ROI of ITAM Implementation

The return on investment for ITAM comes from multiple sources:

  • License optimization: Reclaiming unused licenses typically saves 15-25% of software spend
  • Maintenance elimination: Cancelling maintenance on retired equipment saves 2-5% of IT budget
  • Avoided penalties: Compliance documentation prevents regulatory fines and vendor audit penalties
  • Efficiency gains: Faster support, better procurement, and reduced manual tracking effort

For most mid-market organizations, ITAM pays for itself within 12-18 months through cost savings alone, before considering risk reduction and compliance benefits.

Japan-Specific ITAM Challenges

Implementing ITAM in Japan presents unique challenges that don't exist in other markets. Understanding these challenges is essential for building an effective program.

APPI Compliance Requirements

The Act on Protection of Personal Information creates specific obligations for tracking IT assets that contain personal data. Under APPI, organizations must:

Know what equipment contains personal information: This requires asset classification that identifies which assets store, process, or transmit personal data. A laptop used by HR has different compliance requirements than a conference room display.

Track data handling throughout the lifecycle: APPI requires that you can demonstrate proper handling of personal data from collection through destruction. This means tracking not just that you have a laptop, but what data is on it, how it's protected, and how it's destroyed when retired.

Maintain audit trails: Regulators may ask for documentation showing that equipment containing personal data was properly tracked and destroyed. ITAM provides this audit trail.

Respond to breaches: If a laptop containing personal data is lost or stolen, APPI requires prompt notification. ITAM enables quick identification of what data was on the device so appropriate notifications can be made.

My Number System Tracking

Japan's national identification system creates additional tracking requirements. Any equipment that stores or processes My Number data requires enhanced handling:

Enhanced security controls: My Number data must be stored on equipment with specific security controls, including encryption and access logging. ITAM tracks which assets meet these requirements.

Usage limitation tracking: My Number data can only be used for specific permitted purposes. ITAM helps ensure that equipment containing My Number data is only used appropriately.

Access logging: Systems processing My Number data must maintain access logs. ITAM tracks which assets have logging enabled and ensures logs are maintained.

Destruction documentation: When equipment containing My Number data is retired, enhanced destruction documentation is required. ITAM ensures this documentation is complete and retained.

Mixed Japanese/English Environments

International companies in Japan operate in bilingual environments that create ITAM complexity:

Asset naming conventions: Should assets be named in English, Japanese, or both? Inconsistent naming makes tracking difficult. A laptop might be called "Laptop-Tokyo-001" in one system and "東京ノートPC-001" in another, making it appear as two different assets.

Documentation languages: ITAM documentation may need to be available in both languages for different stakeholders. Global teams need English documentation; local auditors may prefer Japanese.

Vendor integration: Japanese vendors may provide asset information in Japanese formats that don't integrate well with global ITAM systems. Data import and normalization becomes a significant challenge.

Multi-region reporting: Global headquarters wants consolidated reports in English; Japanese operations need local reports that may include Japanese-specific fields. ITAM systems must support both.

Integration with Global Asset Management Systems

International companies often have global ITAM systems that need to incorporate Japan operations:

Data synchronization: Asset data from Japan needs to sync with global systems, but time zones, language differences, and network latency create challenges.

Standardization vs. localization: Global standards for asset categories, naming conventions, and data fields may not accommodate Japan-specific requirements. Finding the right balance is difficult.

Compliance reporting: Japan-specific compliance reports may not fit global reporting templates. Custom reports may be needed.

Vendor management: Global ITAM systems may not integrate with Japanese vendors' systems, requiring manual data entry or custom integrations.

Vendor Ecosystem Complexity

Japan's IT vendor ecosystem is complex and relationship-driven:

Multiple vendor relationships: Organizations often work with multiple vendors for different categories—one for laptops, another for networking, another for servers. Each may have different asset tagging and tracking systems.

Consignment inventory: Some vendors provide equipment on consignment, meaning you don't own it until you use it. Tracking these assets separately from owned assets is essential.

Lease arrangements: Leased equipment requires different tracking than purchased equipment—you need to track lease terms, return dates, and end-of-lease options.

Maintenance contracts: Multiple maintenance contracts with different vendors create complexity. Knowing what's covered, when contracts expire, and how to renew requires systematic tracking.

The IT Asset Lifecycle: Japan Context

Effective ITAM manages assets through their complete lifecycle. In Japan, each phase has specific considerations.

Phase 1: Planning and Procurement

The lifecycle begins with planning what assets to acquire. In Japan, this phase includes:

Requirements gathering: Understanding what the business needs, including Japan-specific requirements like Japanese keyboard layouts, local warranty support, and compatibility with Japanese business systems.

Vendor selection: Choosing vendors who can provide local support, Japanese documentation, and compliance with Japanese regulations. For international companies, this often means balancing global vendor relationships with local capabilities.

Procurement standards: Establishing standard configurations that meet both global and local requirements. This might mean specifying laptops that can be imaged with both global and Japan-specific software loads.

Budget planning: Understanding total cost of ownership, including not just purchase price but also maintenance, support, and end-of-life disposal costs. In Japan, disposal costs can be significant due to proper ITAD requirements.

Phase 2: Acquisition and Receipt

When assets are purchased, proper tracking begins:

Asset tagging: Assigning unique identifiers and physical tags. In Japan, this should include both English and Japanese information for bilingual environments. Tags should be durable and placed where they won't be obscured.

Receipt and inspection: Verifying that received equipment matches what was ordered and is in good condition. Documenting any discrepancies immediately.

Initial configuration: Installing standard images, joining to domain, configuring security settings. In Japan, this includes Japan-specific configurations like time zones, language settings, and local software.

Inventory recording: Adding assets to the ITAM system with complete information including serial numbers, specifications, purchase dates, warranty information, and cost.

Phase 3: Deployment and Assignment

Getting assets into users' hands:

User assignment: Recording who receives each asset. In Japan, this is important for compliance—if a laptop contains personal data, you need to know who has it.

Location tracking: Recording where assets are deployed. For organizations with multiple Tokyo locations or regional offices, this is essential for support and audit purposes.

Configuration customization: Adding user-specific software and settings while maintaining standard security configurations.

Documentation: Providing users with information about their equipment, including support contacts, acceptable use policies, and return procedures.

Phase 4: Operation and Maintenance

The longest phase of the lifecycle:

Ongoing tracking: Maintaining accurate records as assets move, are upgraded, or change configuration. If a laptop gets a new hard drive or additional memory, the ITAM record should be updated.

Maintenance management: Tracking warranty status, maintenance contracts, and repair history. Ensuring that critical assets remain under maintenance.

Support integration: Using ITAM data to speed support. If a user calls with a laptop problem, the support technician should be able to quickly see the model, configuration, and warranty status.

Compliance monitoring: Ensuring that assets containing sensitive data remain compliant with APPI and other regulations throughout their operational life.

Phase 5: Refresh and Upgrade

Planning for replacement:

Lifecycle planning: Establishing standard refresh cycles based on equipment type and usage. Laptops might be refreshed every 3-4 years; servers every 4-5 years.

Budget forecasting: Using ITAM data to forecast upcoming refresh needs and budget requirements. If you have 50 laptops purchased in 2023, you can forecast refresh costs for 2026-2027.

Technology evaluation: Assessing whether new technology justifies early refresh. The move to Windows 11, for example, may accelerate refresh timelines for older hardware.

User communication: Notifying users of upcoming refreshes and managing expectations about timing and process.

Phase 6: Disposal and ITAD

The final phase requires careful handling:

Data sanitization: Ensuring all data is properly destroyed before equipment leaves organizational control. This is critical for APPI compliance.

Asset recovery: Identifying equipment with residual value that can be resold or repurposed. ITAM data helps determine which equipment is suitable for resale.

Disposal documentation: Creating certificates of destruction and other documentation needed for compliance and audit purposes.

Financial closure: Updating financial records to reflect asset disposal and any recovery value.

ITAM record closure: Marking assets as disposed in the ITAM system while retaining records for compliance purposes.

Building Your ITAM Program: Step-by-Step

Implementing ITAM is a significant undertaking. This phased approach helps you build systematically.

Phase 1: Discovery and Baseline Inventory

Start by understanding what you have:

Automated discovery: Use network scanning tools to identify connected devices. Tools like Lansweeper, ServiceNow Discovery, or Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager can identify a significant portion of your assets automatically.

Physical audit: Complement automated discovery with physical audits. Walk through offices, data centers, and storage areas to identify assets that automated tools missed—offline devices, spare equipment, or assets on isolated networks.

User surveys: Ask employees to report what equipment they have. This catches assets that might be missed by other methods.

Vendor records: Review purchase records from vendors to identify assets that should be in your environment.

The goal is a complete baseline inventory that you can use to build your ITAM system. Don't worry about perfection—aim for 90-95% coverage, then improve over time.

Phase 2: Policy and Procedure Development

Establish the framework for ongoing ITAM:

Asset classification: Define categories for different types of assets and their data sensitivity. A laptop used by finance has different handling requirements than a conference room display.

Naming conventions: Establish standard naming conventions that work in your bilingual environment. Consider using numeric asset tags that are language-neutral.

Data requirements: Define what information must be collected for each asset type. All assets need basic information (type, model, serial number, purchase date); some need additional data (warranty end date, maintenance contract, data classification).

Lifecycle procedures: Document procedures for each phase of the asset lifecycle—from procurement through disposal. Who does what, when, and how is it documented?

Roles and responsibilities: Define who is responsible for ITAM activities. This includes asset managers who maintain the system, procurement staff who enter new assets, support staff who update records, and finance staff who handle depreciation.

Phase 3: Tool Selection and Implementation

Choose and implement ITAM software:

Requirements definition: Based on your policies, define what you need in an ITAM tool. Must-haves versus nice-to-haves. Consider integration requirements with existing systems.

Vendor evaluation: Evaluate ITAM tools against your requirements. For Japan-based organizations, consider:

  • Lansweeper: Good for SMBs, strong discovery, reasonable cost
  • ServiceNow: Enterprise-grade, excellent for large organizations
  • Freshservice: Mid-market option, good balance of features and cost
  • Snipe-IT: Open source, free but limited Japan support

Implementation: Configure the tool, import your baseline inventory, and establish integrations with other systems (procurement, finance, support ticketing).

Data migration: Clean and migrate existing asset data. This is often the most time-consuming part of implementation—expect to spend significant effort normalizing data from various sources.

Phase 4: Process Integration and Training

Make ITAM part of daily operations:

Workflow integration: Integrate ITAM activities into existing workflows. Procurement should enter assets into ITAM as part of the purchasing process. Support should update ITAM when assets are moved or changed. Disposal should trigger ITAM updates.

Training: Train all staff who interact with assets on ITAM procedures. This includes IT staff, procurement, finance, and end users who need to know how to report asset issues or returns.

Documentation: Create user guides, quick reference cards, and other documentation that makes ITAM procedures easy to follow.

Communication: Communicate the importance of ITAM to the organization. Help people understand why accurate tracking matters for compliance and cost management.

Phase 5: Ongoing Optimization and Auditing

Continuous improvement:

Regular audits: Conduct periodic audits to verify ITAM accuracy. Compare physical inventory to system records. Identify and correct discrepancies.

Metrics and reporting: Track ITAM metrics like inventory accuracy, time to add new assets, and compliance with procedures. Use these metrics to identify improvement opportunities.

Process refinement: Based on audit findings and user feedback, refine procedures to make ITAM more efficient and accurate.

Technology updates: Keep ITAM tools current. Update software, refresh integrations, and take advantage of new features.

ITAM Software Options for Japan-Based Businesses

Selecting the right ITAM tool is critical for success. Here's how major options compare for Japan-based organizations.

Lansweeper

Best for: Small to medium businesses (up to 500 assets)

Strengths:

  • Strong automated discovery of Windows, Mac, Linux, and network devices
  • Agentless scanning reduces deployment complexity
  • Good reporting and dashboard capabilities
  • Reasonable cost (starting around ¥150,000/year for small deployments)
  • Active community and good documentation

Japan considerations:

  • Interface is English-only, which may be challenging for some Japanese staff
  • No native integration with Japanese accounting systems
  • Discovery may miss some Japan-specific device types

Cost: ¥150,000-500,000/year depending on asset count

ServiceNow

Best for: Large enterprises (1000+ assets)

Strengths:

  • Comprehensive ITAM capabilities integrated with broader ITSM platform
  • Excellent workflow automation
  • Strong integration capabilities
  • Enterprise-grade security and compliance features
  • Good multi-language support including Japanese

Japan considerations:

  • Strong Japan presence with local support
  • Can integrate with Japanese business systems
  • Higher cost may be prohibitive for smaller organizations

Cost: ¥2,000,000+ annually for mid-size deployments

Freshservice

Best for: Mid-market organizations (100-1000 assets)

Strengths:

  • Good balance of features and cost
  • Modern, intuitive interface
  • Strong ITSM integration (ticketing, change management)
  • Reasonable multi-language support
  • Cloud-based with minimal infrastructure requirements

Japan considerations:

  • Interface available in Japanese
  • Good documentation and support
  • May require customization for Japan-specific requirements

Cost: ¥500,000-1,500,000/year depending on features and users

Snipe-IT

Best for: Organizations with limited budgets

Strengths:

  • Open source and free to use
  • Good basic asset tracking
  • Active community
  • Customizable

Japan considerations:

  • No official Japan support
  • Interface is English-only
  • Requires technical expertise to implement and maintain
  • Limited integration capabilities

Cost: Free (self-hosted) or ¥50,000-100,000/year (cloud-hosted)

Key ITAM Metrics to Track

Effective ITAM programs track metrics that demonstrate value and identify improvement opportunities:

Asset utilization rate: What percentage of assets are actively used? Low utilization suggests over-purchasing or poor allocation. Target: 85%+ for user-facing equipment.

Total cost of ownership (TCO): What's the full cost of each asset category including purchase, maintenance, support, and disposal? Use this for budgeting and vendor evaluation.

Compliance audit results: How do you perform on compliance audits? Track findings and remediation to demonstrate improvement.

Incident rates by asset type: Are certain models or vendors more prone to problems? Use this data for procurement decisions.

Refresh cycle optimization: Are you refreshing assets at optimal intervals? Too early wastes money; too late increases risk and support costs.

Inventory accuracy: How well does your ITAM system match physical reality? Regular audits should show 95%+ accuracy.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing IT asset management services for international companies operating in Japan. We help organizations implement ITAM programs that satisfy both global standards and Japan-specific compliance requirements including APPI and My Number tracking. From tool selection and implementation to ongoing management, we provide the expertise you need to control costs and reduce risk. Contact us for a free ITAM assessment.

---

Related Articles

  • IT Lifecycle Management Tokyo: From Procurement to Disposal
  • Software Asset Management Japan: License Compliance and Cost Optimization
  • IT Inventory Management Japan: Best Practices for Accuracy

IT Asset Management Japan: Complete Framework for Compliance and Efficiency

When a mid-sized pharmaceutical company in Tokyo faced their annual audit in 2025, the IT director thought they were prepared. The audit revealed what the IT director had suspected but couldn't quantify: their IT asset management was inadequate for a regulated industry in Japan. They were over-licensed on several software platforms, under-licensed on others, had equipment that was no longer under maintenance but still in production, and lacked the documentation needed to demonstrate APPI compliance for data handling. This story illustrates why IT Asset Management (ITAM) matters—and why it's particularly challenging in Japan's regulatory environment. This guide provides a comprehensive framework for building an ITAM program that ensures compliance, optimizes costs, and reduces risk.

What Is IT Asset Management and Why It Matters

IT Asset Management is the set of business practices that join financial, contractual, and inventory functions to support life cycle management and strategic decision making for the IT environment. In simpler terms, ITAM is knowing what IT assets you have, where they are, how they're used, and how much they cost—then using that information to make better decisions.

The scope of ITAM includes hardware assets (servers, laptops, networking equipment, mobile devices), software assets (licenses, subscriptions, cloud services), and the relationships between them. A comprehensive ITAM program tracks assets from procurement through deployment, operation, maintenance, and final disposition.

Cost: ¥2,000,000+ annually for mid-size deployments

Freshservice

Best for: Mid-market organizations (100-1000 assets)

Strengths:

  • Good balance of features and cost
  • Modern, intuitive interface
  • Strong ITSM integration (ticketing, change management)
  • Reasonable multi-language support
  • Cloud-based with minimal infrastructure requirements

Japan considerations:

  • Interface available in Japanese
  • Good documentation and support
  • May require customization for Japan-specific requirements

Cost: ¥500,000-1,500,000/year depending on features and users

Snipe-IT

Best for: Organizations with limited budgets

Strengths:

  • Open source and free to use
  • Good basic asset tracking
  • Active community
  • Customizable

Japan considerations:

  • No official Japan support
  • Interface is English-only
  • Requires technical expertise to implement and maintain
  • Limited integration capabilities

Cost: Free (self-hosted) or ¥50,000-100,000/year (cloud-hosted)

Key ITAM Metrics to Track

Effective ITAM programs track metrics that demonstrate value and identify improvement opportunities:

Asset utilization rate: What percentage of assets are actively used? Low utilization suggests over-purchasing or poor allocation. Target: 85%+ for user-facing equipment.

Total cost of ownership (TCO): What's the full cost of each asset category including purchase, maintenance, support, and disposal? Use this for budgeting and vendor evaluation.

Compliance audit results: How do you perform on compliance audits? Track findings and remediation to demonstrate improvement.

Incident rates by asset type: Are certain models or vendors more prone to problems? Use this data for procurement decisions.

Refresh cycle optimization: Are you refreshing assets at optimal intervals? Too early wastes money; too late increases risk and support costs.

Inventory accuracy: How well does your ITAM system match physical reality? Regular audits should show 95%+ accuracy.

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing IT asset management services for international companies operating in Japan. We help organizations implement ITAM programs that satisfy both global standards and Japan-specific compliance requirements including APPI and My Number tracking. From tool selection and implementation to ongoing management, we provide the expertise you need to control costs and reduce risk. Contact us for a free ITAM assessment.

title: English Version: Software Asset Management Japan: License Compliance and Cost Optimization
excerpt: English version: Optimize your software licensing in Tokyo. Learn about Microsoft 365 license optimization, SaaS management, compliance auditing, and avoiding the risks of shadow IT in Japan.
htmlContent:
This page is an auto-localized English version generated from the original Japanese article.

Software Asset Management Japan: License Compliance and Cost Optimization

A Tokyo-based consulting firm discovered during a routine internal review in 2025 that they were paying for 43 Microsoft 365 E3 licenses that hadn't been used in over six months. Former employees had left, but their licenses were never reclaimed. Contractors had finished projects, but their accounts remained active. And a department that switched to Google Workspace still had their Microsoft licenses running in parallel "just in case." The annual cost of these unused licenses: ¥2.3 million. The firm had been throwing away enough money each year to hire an additional staff member, simply because they lacked systematic software asset management.

This story is far from unique. Industry analysts estimate that organizations waste 20-30% of their software spend on unused or underutilized licenses. In Japan's complex regulatory environment, the risks extend beyond cost to include compliance violations, audit penalties, and security gaps. This guide explains how to implement effective Software Asset Management (SAM) in Tokyo's unique business context.

What Is Software Asset Management (SAM)?

Software Asset Management is the business practice that manages and optimizes the purchase, deployment, maintenance, utilization, and disposal of software applications within an organization. While IT Asset Management (ITAM) tracks hardware and software together, SAM focuses specifically on software licenses, subscriptions, and usage.

Difference Between SAM and ITAM

ITAM is broader, tracking all IT assets including hardware, software, and network equipment. SAM is a subset of ITAM focused specifically on software. While ITAM might track that you have a laptop with asset tag 12345, SAM tracks that the laptop has a Windows license, an Office 365 subscription, and Adobe Creative Cloud installed, along with whether those licenses are properly allocated and used.

For effective software management, you need both. ITAM provides the foundation of knowing what equipment you have; SAM builds on that to ensure the software on that equipment is properly licensed and used.

Business Benefits of SAM

The benefits of effective SAM extend across the organization:

Cost reduction: The most immediate benefit is eliminating waste. Reclaiming unused licenses, consolidating redundant applications, and optimizing license tiers typically saves 15-25% of software spend. For an organization spending ¥10 million annually on software, that's ¥1.5-2.5 million in savings.

Compliance assurance: Software vendors are increasingly aggressive about license compliance. Microsoft, Adobe, Oracle, and others conduct regular audits, and penalties for non-compliance can be substantial—often three times the cost of the licenses you should have purchased. SAM ensures you're prepared for audits and compliant with licensing terms.

Security improvement: Unmanaged software creates security risks. Shadow IT applications may not be patched or may have vulnerabilities. SAM identifies all software in your environment so it can be properly secured.

Operational efficiency: Standardizing on approved software reduces support complexity and improves interoperability. SAM helps identify and eliminate redundant or incompatible applications.

Strategic planning: Understanding your software portfolio enables strategic decisions about technology direction, vendor relationships, and investment priorities.

Legal Risks of Poor SAM

The risks of inadequate SAM are significant:

Vendor audit penalties: If a software audit finds you're under-licensed, you'll be required to purchase the missing licenses, often at list price rather than discounted prices you might have negotiated. Additionally, vendors may impose penalties of 2-3x the license cost as a "fine" for non-compliance.

Copyright infringement: Using software without proper licenses is copyright infringement. While criminal prosecution of businesses is rare, civil liability is real, and vendors have become more aggressive about legal action.

Reputational damage: Being publicly identified as a software license violator damages your organization's reputation, particularly for public companies or those in regulated industries.

Security and compliance gaps: Unmanaged software may not be patched, may violate security policies, and may create compliance issues. If you don't know what software is in your environment, you can't secure it properly.

Software Licensing in Japan: Key Considerations

Japan's software licensing environment has unique characteristics that affect SAM.

Japanese vs. International Licensing Terms

Software vendors often have different licensing terms for Japan:

Pricing: Japanese pricing may differ from US or European pricing due to market conditions, currency fluctuations, and local competitive factors. Don't assume global pricing applies in Japan.

Product availability: Some software products or features may not be available in Japan due to localization requirements, regulatory restrictions, or market decisions.

Support terms: Support hours, response times, and language availability may differ in Japan. Ensure you understand what support is available in Japanese versus English.

Subscription models: Cloud and subscription licensing may have different terms in Japan. Data residency requirements, for example, may affect where cloud services can be hosted.

Volume Licensing Programs

Major vendors offer volume licensing programs in Japan:

Microsoft: The Microsoft Products and Services Agreement (MPSA) and Enterprise Agreement (EA) provide volume licensing for organizations of various sizes. These programs offer discounted pricing but require minimum purchase commitments and true-up reporting.

Adobe: Adobe's Volume Licensing program provides centralized license management and discounted pricing for Creative Cloud and Document Cloud products.

Other vendors: Most major software vendors offer similar programs. Understanding the specific terms, benefits, and obligations of each is essential for SAM.

Cloud/SaaS Subscription Management

Cloud software creates new SAM challenges:

Recurring billing: Unlike perpetual licenses with upfront costs, SaaS subscriptions create ongoing monthly or annual expenses that need to be tracked and managed.

Auto-renewal: Many SaaS subscriptions auto-renew unless cancelled, which can lead to paying for services you no longer need.

Usage-based pricing: Some cloud services charge based on usage (storage, compute, transactions) rather than fixed per-user fees. These require ongoing monitoring to control costs.

Shadow IT: Cloud services can be procured directly by users with corporate credit cards, bypassing IT oversight. This creates both cost and security risks.

Currency and Billing Considerations

For international companies, currency affects SAM:

Exchange rate exposure: If your budget is in one currency but software is billed in another, exchange rate fluctuations affect costs. A strong yen reduces costs; a weak yen increases them.

Billing timing: Annual subscriptions billed upfront create large cash outflows. Monthly billing spreads costs but may be more expensive overall.

Tax treatment: Software may be subject to different tax treatment depending on how it's licensed and where it's delivered. Consumption tax (消費税) applies to most software purchases in Japan.

Support Language Requirements

Language support affects software selection and licensing:

Japanese language support: Business-critical applications should have Japanese language interfaces and Japanese-language support. This is essential for user adoption and productivity.

English language support: For international companies, English support is also important for global coordination and for expat employees.

Documentation: Consider whether documentation, training materials, and support resources are available in the languages your users need.

Common Software Compliance Risks for Japan Businesses

Understanding common compliance risks helps you focus your SAM efforts.

Under-Licensing: Using More Than Purchased

Under-licensing occurs when you have more users or installations than your licenses permit:

User growth: As organizations grow, they add users but may not add corresponding licenses. A company that purchases 100 licenses and grows to 120 employees may be under-licensed by 20 users.

Installation proliferation: Some licenses permit installation on multiple devices per user, but others don't. Installing software on both a laptop and desktop may require two licenses depending on the product terms.

Virtualization: Virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) and virtual machines create licensing complexity. You may need licenses for both the host and virtual instances, depending on the software.

Contractor and temporary worker usage: Using software on behalf of contractors or temporary workers may require licenses even if those users aren't employees.

The risk of under-licensing is audit failure and penalties. Software vendors have become increasingly sophisticated about detecting under-licensing through phone-home features, usage analytics, and audit tools.

Over-Licensing: Paying for Unused Licenses

Over-licensing occurs when you purchase more licenses than you need:

Shelfware: Licenses that are purchased but never deployed. This often happens with enterprise agreements that include minimum purchase commitments.

Departing employees: When employees leave, their software licenses may not be reclaimed. Over time, this creates a growing pool of unused licenses.

Redundant applications: Purchasing licenses for multiple applications that serve the same purpose. If you have both Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, you may be paying twice for similar functionality.

License tier mismatch: Purchasing higher-tier licenses than needed. A user who only needs email and basic Office apps may be assigned an E3 license when an E1 or Business Basic would suffice.

Over-licensing doesn't create legal risk, but it wastes money that could be used elsewhere.

Shadow IT and Unauthorized Software

Shadow IT refers to software that's used without IT's knowledge or approval:

User-procured software: Users purchasing software with corporate credit cards or installing free tools without approval. This creates both cost and security risks.

Free software: Users installing free software that may have malware, adware, or problematic licensing terms. "Free" doesn't always mean safe or legal for business use.

Personal software: Users installing personal software on corporate devices. This may violate policies and create licensing confusion.

Cloud services: Users signing up for cloud services with corporate email addresses, creating corporate accounts that IT doesn't know about.

Shadow IT is particularly challenging in Japan, where users may install Japanese-language tools that global IT teams aren't aware of.

Employee-Installed Applications

Even well-intentioned employees can create compliance issues:

Open source software: Open source isn't always free for commercial use. Some licenses require attribution, some prohibit commercial use, and some require that derivative works also be open source.

Trial software: Users installing trial versions that expire but remain installed. This creates both compliance and security risks.

Unlicensed copies: Users installing software they have licenses for at home, or that they "borrowed" from a friend. This is copyright infringement.

Virtualization and Licensing Complexity

Virtual environments create licensing complexity:

Server virtualization: Virtual machines may require separate licenses for the operating system, applications, and management tools. The licensing model depends on whether you're using VMware, Hyper-V, or other platforms.

Desktop virtualization: VDI environments like Citrix or VMware Horizon have specific licensing requirements that differ from physical desktops.

Containerization: Docker and Kubernetes environments have their own licensing considerations, particularly for orchestration and management tools.

Audit Exposure and Penalties

Software vendors conduct audits to ensure compliance:

Audit triggers: Vendors may audit based on license purchases, support requests, or random selection. Declining revenue in a territory may also trigger increased audit activity.

Audit process: Audits typically involve running discovery tools to inventory software installations, reviewing purchase records, and comparing the two to identify gaps.

Penalty structure: If under-licensing is found, you'll be required to purchase the missing licenses, often at list price. Penalties of 2-3x the license cost are common. For willful infringement, criminal penalties may apply.

Defense strategies: Good SAM practices are the best defense. Maintain accurate records, understand your license terms, and address gaps proactively before an audit finds them.

Building a SAM Program: Step-by-Step

Implementing SAM is a systematic process.

Step 1: Discovery - Inventory All Software Installations

You can't manage what you don't know about:

Automated discovery: Use software inventory tools to discover what's installed on your network. Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager, Lansweeper, and ServiceNow all have software discovery capabilities.

Agent-based vs. agentless: Agent-based discovery provides more detailed information but requires software installation. Agentless discovery is less intrusive but may miss some installations.

Cloud service discovery: Don't forget cloud services. Tools like Microsoft Cloud App Security or third-party CASBs (Cloud Access Security Brokers) can identify shadow IT cloud services.

Manual verification: Supplement automated discovery with manual verification. Walk through offices, check standalone machines, and interview users about software they use.

The goal is a complete inventory of all software in your environment—both approved and unapproved, installed and cloud-based.

Step 2: Normalization - Standardize Naming and Versions

Raw discovery data is messy:

Normalization: Software may be discovered with different names for the same product ("Microsoft Office," "Office 365," "Microsoft 365 Apps"). Normalization maps these to a standard name.

Version tracking: Track versions to identify outdated software that needs updating. Microsoft Office 2016, 2019, and 2021 are all different versions with different support status.

Edition identification: Different editions (Home, Professional, Enterprise) have different features and licensing. Ensure you're tracking editions correctly.

Normalization makes it possible to get accurate counts and identify compliance issues.

Step 3: Entitlement - Document All Licenses and Purchases

Know what you own:

License documentation: Gather all license documentation including purchase orders, license agreements, certificate of authenticity, and volume licensing agreements.

Entitlement reconciliation: Create a central record of what you're entitled to use. This should include product names, versions, quantities, license types (perpetual, subscription, user-based, device-based), and purchase dates.

Agreement tracking: Track volume licensing agreements, enterprise agreements, and other contracts. Know when they expire, what the terms are, and what your obligations are.

User assignment: Track which users or devices are assigned to which licenses. This helps identify unused licenses and ensures compliance.

Step 4: Reconciliation - Compare Installations to Entitlements

Find the gaps:

Compliance comparison: Compare your software inventory (what's installed) to your entitlements (what you own). Identify over-licensed and under-licensed products.

Usage analysis: For products with usage tracking, analyze whether installed software is actually being used. A product may be installed and properly licensed but never used.

Prioritization: Prioritize compliance issues based on risk. Under-licensing of high-value products from vendors who audit aggressively is higher priority than over-licensing of inexpensive tools.

Step 5: Optimization - Right-Size Licensing

Eliminate waste and address gaps:

License reclamation: Identify and reclaim unused licenses. When employees leave, terminate their software subscriptions. When departments stop using applications, cancel the licenses.

License downgrades: If users have higher-tier licenses than they need, downgrade them to appropriate tiers. A user who only needs email doesn't need an E5 license.

Consolidation: Identify redundant applications and consolidate on standard platforms. If you have three project management tools, standardize on one.

Procurement optimization: Use your SAM data to negotiate better pricing. Volume discounts, enterprise agreements, and competitive bidding can all reduce costs.

Step 6: Governance - Policies and Ongoing Management

Maintain compliance over time:

Procurement policies: Establish policies that require IT approval for software purchases. This prevents shadow IT and ensures proper licensing.

Onboarding procedures: Include software provisioning in employee onboarding. New employees should receive standard software packages with proper licensing.

Offboarding procedures: Include software license reclamation in employee offboarding. When employees leave, their licenses should be terminated or transferred.

Regular audits: Conduct regular internal audits to maintain compliance. Quarterly reviews help catch issues before they become major problems.

Microsoft 365 License Optimization for Tokyo Businesses

Microsoft 365 is one of the largest software expenses for most organizations, and optimization can yield significant savings.

Plan Comparison: Business vs. Enterprise

Microsoft offers multiple M365 plans with different features and pricing:

Microsoft 365 Business plans:

  • Business Basic (¥1,100/user/month): Web and mobile apps only, no desktop Office
  • Business Standard (¥1,874/user/month): Desktop Office plus web/mobile
  • Business Premium (¥2,860/user/month): Adds advanced security and device management

Microsoft 365 Enterprise plans:

  • E3 (¥5,200/user/month): Full desktop Office, Exchange, SharePoint, Teams
  • E5 (¥8,400/user/month): Adds advanced security, analytics, and voice capabilities

Note: Microsoft announced price increases effective July 2026. Business Standard will increase from $12.50 to $14 USD per user per month.

For most organizations, Business Standard or E3 are the sweet spots. Business Basic is too limited for most users; E5 is expensive and many organizations don't use the advanced features.

Add-On Optimization

Microsoft offers numerous add-ons that can be purchased separately:

Phone System and Calling Plans: If you need PSTN calling, compare Microsoft's calling plans to third-party options. Microsoft's plans are convenient but may be more expensive.

Advanced Threat Protection: ATP is included in Business Premium and E5 but can be added to lower tiers. Evaluate whether you need the advanced protection or if standard security suffices.

Power BI Pro: Included in E5 but must be purchased separately for other plans. Evaluate whether users actually need Power BI or if Excel is sufficient.

User Profiling for Right-Sizing

Not all users need the same license tier:

Light users: Users who only need email and occasional document viewing may be fine with Business Basic or even Exchange Online Plan 1 (¥400/user/month).

Standard users: Most office workers need Business Standard or E3 for full desktop Office applications.

Power users: Users who need advanced analytics, security, or voice capabilities may warrant E5.

Shared resources: Conference rooms, shared workstations, and kiosks may need special licensing. Microsoft offers specific licenses for these scenarios.

Cost Savings Opportunities

Typical savings opportunities include:

Downgrading unused E5 licenses: Organizations often assign E5 to everyone when only some users need the advanced features. Downgrading users who don't use E5 features can save ¥2,500/user/month.

Eliminating duplicate services: If you have both Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, standardize on one. Paying for both is wasteful.

Optimizing add-ons: Review all add-ons to ensure they're being used. Unused add-ons should be cancelled.

Annual vs. monthly billing: Annual billing is typically 15-20% cheaper than monthly. If cash flow permits, switch to annual.

SaaS Management: The Growing Challenge

Cloud software creates new SAM challenges.

Shadow SaaS Discovery

Finding cloud services that users have signed up for:

Cloud access security brokers (CASBs): Tools like Microsoft Cloud App Security, Netskope, or McAfee MVISION Cloud can identify cloud services being accessed from your network.

Expense report analysis: Review expense reports for software subscriptions. Users may be expensing SaaS subscriptions that IT doesn't know about.

Email domain monitoring: Monitor for corporate email addresses being used to sign up for cloud services. Many services send confirmation emails that can be detected.

Financial audits: Regular audits of corporate credit card statements can reveal SaaS subscriptions.

Subscription Tracking and Renewal Management

Managing ongoing SaaS subscriptions:

Central tracking: Maintain a central inventory of all SaaS subscriptions including cost, renewal date, owner, and business justification.

Renewal alerts: Set alerts 30-60 days before renewals so you can evaluate whether to continue, modify, or cancel.

Usage reviews: Regularly review whether SaaS services are being used. Low usage may indicate the service isn't needed.

Contract consolidation: Where possible, consolidate SaaS contracts to improve negotiating position and simplify management.

Usage Analytics and Optimization

Understanding how SaaS is being used:

Built-in analytics: Most SaaS platforms provide usage analytics. Review these to understand actual usage patterns.

License tier optimization: Many SaaS services have multiple tiers. Ensure users are on appropriate tiers based on their usage.

Seat optimization: Remove users who aren't using the service. Many organizations pay for "seats" that are never used.

Security and Access Control

Ensuring SaaS services are secure:

Single sign-on (SSO): Where possible, use SSO rather than separate passwords for each service. This improves security and simplifies user management.

Multi-factor authentication: Require MFA for all SaaS services, especially those containing sensitive data.

Access reviews: Regularly review who has access to what SaaS services. Remove access for users who no longer need it.

Integration with Identity Management

Connecting SaaS to your identity infrastructure:

Directory integration: Integrate SaaS services with your directory (Azure AD, Okta, etc.) for centralized user management.

Automated provisioning: Use automated provisioning to create and remove accounts based on directory membership. This ensures that when employees leave, their SaaS access is automatically terminated.

Role-based access: Implement role-based access control so users get appropriate access based on their job function.

SAM Tools for Japan-Based Organizations

Tools can significantly improve SAM effectiveness.

Global Solutions

Flexera: Enterprise-grade SAM with strong discovery, normalization, and optimization capabilities. Good for large organizations with complex environments.

Snow Software: Comprehensive SAM platform with strong cloud service management. Good for organizations with significant cloud software usage.

ServiceNow: ITSM platform with SAM capabilities integrated into broader IT management. Good for organizations already using ServiceNow for ITSM.

Microsoft-Specific Tools

Microsoft 365 Admin Center: Built-in tools for managing Microsoft 365 licenses, including usage reports and license assignment.

Azure AD: Identity management with reporting on application usage and access.

Microsoft Cloud App Security: CASB for discovering and managing cloud services.

Considerations for Mixed JP/EN Environments

For international companies in Japan:

Multi-language support: Ensure SAM tools support both Japanese and English for user interfaces, reports, and documentation.

Local integration: Consider whether tools can integrate with Japanese business systems like accounting software or HR systems.

Support availability: Ensure vendors provide support in appropriate languages and time zones for your needs.

Cost-Benefit Analysis

SAM tools represent an investment:

Tool costs: Enterprise SAM tools can cost ¥1-5 million annually depending on organization size. This needs to be weighed against expected savings.

Implementation costs: Implementing SAM tools requires time and effort. Factor in implementation costs when evaluating ROI.

Ongoing effort: Tools require ongoing maintenance and management. Ensure you have resources to maintain the tool effectively.

DIY vs. tool: For smaller organizations, spreadsheet-based SAM may be sufficient. As you grow, tools become more valuable.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing software asset management services for international companies operating in Japan. We help organizations optimize their Microsoft 365 licensing, manage SaaS subscriptions, and maintain compliance with software vendor requirements. Our bilingual team understands both global software licensing and Japan-specific considerations. Contact us for a free SAM assessment.

---

Related Articles

  • IT Asset Management Japan: Complete Framework for Compliance
  • IT Lifecycle Management Tokyo: From Procurement to Disposal
  • IT Inventory Management Japan: Best Practices for Accuracy

Free software: Users installing free software that may have malware, adware, or problematic licensing terms. "Free" doesn't always mean safe or legal for business use.

Personal software: Users installing personal software on corporate devices. This may violate policies and create licensing confusion.

Cloud services: Users signing up for cloud services with corporate email addresses, creating corporate accounts that IT doesn't know about.

Shadow IT is particularly challenging in Japan, where users may install Japanese-language tools that global IT teams aren't aware of.

Employee-Installed Applications

Even well-intentioned employees can create compliance issues:

Open source software: Open source isn't always free for commercial use. Some licenses require attribution, some prohibit commercial use, and some require that derivative works also be open source.

Trial software: Users installing trial versions that expire but remain installed. This creates both compliance and security risks.

Unlicensed copies: Users installing software they have licenses for at home, or that they "borrowed" from a friend. This is copyright infringement.

Virtualization and Licensing Complexity

Virtual environments create licensing complexity:

Server virtualization: Virtual machines may require separate licenses for the operating system, applications, and management tools. The licensing model depends on whether you're using VMware, Hyper-V, or other platforms.

Desktop virtualization: VDI environments like Citrix or VMware Horizon have specific licensing requirements that differ from physical desktops.

Containerization: Docker and Kubernetes environments have their own licensing considerations, particularly for orchestration and management tools.

Audit Exposure and Penalties

Software vendors conduct audits to ensure compliance:

Audit triggers: Vendors may audit based on license purchases, support requests, or random selection. Declining revenue in a territory may also trigger increased audit activity.

Audit process: Audits typically involve running discovery tools to inventory software installations, reviewing purchase records, and comparing the two to identify gaps.

Penalty structure: If under-licensing is found, you'll be required to purchase the missing licenses, often at list price. Penalties of 2-3x the license cost are common. For willful infringement, criminal penalties may apply.

Defense strategies: Good SAM practices are the best defense. Maintain accurate records, understand your license terms, and address gaps proactively before an audit finds them.

Building a SAM Program: Step-by-Step

Implementing SAM is a systematic process.

Step 1: Discovery - Inventory All Software Installations

You can't manage what you don't know about:

Automated discovery: Use software inventory tools to discover what's installed on your network. Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager, Lansweeper, and ServiceNow all have software discovery capabilities.

Agent-based vs. agentless: Agent-based discovery provides more detailed information but requires software installation. Agentless discovery is less intrusive but may miss some installations.

Cloud service discovery: Don't forget cloud services. Tools like Microsoft Cloud App Security or third-party CASBs (Cloud Access Security Brokers) can identify shadow IT cloud services.

Manual verification: Supplement automated discovery with manual verification. Walk through offices, check standalone machines, and interview users about software they use.

The goal is a complete inventory of all software in your environment—both approved and unapproved, installed and cloud-based.

Step 2: Normalization - Standardize Naming and Versions

Raw discovery data is messy:

Normalization: Software may be discovered with different names for the same product ("Microsoft Office," "Office 365," "Microsoft 365 Apps"). Normalization maps these to a standard name.

Version tracking: Track versions to identify outdated software that needs updating. Microsoft Office 2016, 2019, and 2021 are all different versions with different support status.

Edition identification: Different editions (Home, Professional, Enterprise) have different features and licensing. Ensure you're tracking editions correctly.

Normalization makes it possible to get accurate counts and identify compliance issues.

Step 3: Entitlement - Document All Licenses and Purchases

Know what you own:

License documentation: Gather all license documentation including purchase orders, license agreements, certificate of authenticity, and volume licensing agreements.

Entitlement reconciliation: Create a central record of what you're entitled to use. This should include product names, versions, quantities, license types (perpetual, subscription, user-based, device-based), and purchase dates.

Agreement tracking: Track volume licensing agreements, enterprise agreements, and other contracts. Know when they expire, what the terms are, and what your obligations are.

User assignment: Track which users or devices are assigned to which licenses. This helps identify unused licenses and ensures compliance.

Step 4: Reconciliation - Compare Installations to Entitlements

Find the gaps:

Compliance comparison: Compare your software inventory (what's installed) to your entitlements (what you own). Identify over-licensed and under-licensed products.

Usage analysis: For products with usage tracking, analyze whether installed software is actually being used. A product may be installed and properly licensed but never used.

Prioritization: Prioritize compliance issues based on risk. Under-licensing of high-value products from vendors who audit aggressively is higher priority than over-licensing of inexpensive tools.

Step 5: Optimization - Right-Size Licensing

Eliminate waste and address gaps:

License reclamation: Identify and reclaim unused licenses. When employees leave, terminate their software subscriptions. When departments stop using applications, cancel the licenses.

License downgrades: If users have higher-tier licenses than they need, downgrade them to appropriate tiers. A user who only needs email doesn't need an E5 license.

Consolidation: Identify redundant applications and consolidate on standard platforms. If you have three project management tools, standardize on one.

Procurement optimization: Use your SAM data to negotiate better pricing. Volume discounts, enterprise agreements, and competitive bidding can all reduce costs.

Step 6: Governance - Policies and Ongoing Management

Maintain compliance over time:

Procurement policies: Establish policies that require IT approval for software purchases. This prevents shadow IT and ensures proper licensing.

Onboarding procedures: Include software provisioning in employee onboarding. New employees should receive standard software packages with proper licensing.

Offboarding procedures: Include software license reclamation in employee offboarding. When employees leave, their licenses should be terminated or transferred.

Regular audits: Conduct regular internal audits to maintain compliance. Quarterly reviews help catch issues before they become major problems.

Microsoft 365 License Optimization for Tokyo Businesses

Microsoft 365 is one of the largest software expenses for most organizations, and optimization can yield significant savings.

Plan Comparison: Business vs. Enterprise

Microsoft offers multiple M365 plans with different features and pricing:

Microsoft 365 Business plans:

  • Business Basic (¥1,100/user/month): Web and mobile apps only, no desktop Office
  • Business Standard (¥1,874/user/month): Desktop Office plus web/mobile
  • Business Premium (¥2,860/user/month): Adds advanced security and device management

Microsoft 365 Enterprise plans:

  • E3 (¥5,200/user/month): Full desktop Office, Exchange, SharePoint, Teams
  • E5 (¥8,400/user/month): Adds advanced security, analytics, and voice capabilities

Note: Microsoft announced price increases effective July 2026. Business Standard will increase from $12.50 to $14 USD per user per month.

For most organizations, Business Standard or E3 are the sweet spots. Business Basic is too limited for most users; E5 is expensive and many organizations don't use the advanced features.

Add-On Optimization

Microsoft offers numerous add-ons that can be purchased separately:

Phone System and Calling Plans: If you need PSTN calling, compare Microsoft's calling plans to third-party options. Microsoft's plans are convenient but may be more expensive.

Advanced Threat Protection: ATP is included in Business Premium and E5 but can be added to lower tiers. Evaluate whether you need the advanced protection or if standard security suffices.

Power BI Pro: Included in E5 but must be purchased separately for other plans. Evaluate whether users actually need Power BI or if Excel is sufficient.

User Profiling for Right-Sizing

Not all users need the same license tier:

Light users: Users who only need email and occasional document viewing may be fine with Business Basic or even Exchange Online Plan 1 (¥400/user/month).

Standard users: Most office workers need Business Standard or E3 for full desktop Office applications.

Power users: Users who need advanced analytics, security, or voice capabilities may warrant E5.

Shared resources: Conference rooms, shared workstations, and kiosks may need special licensing. Microsoft offers specific licenses for these scenarios.

Cost Savings Opportunities

Typical savings opportunities include:

Downgrading unused E5 licenses: Organizations often assign E5 to everyone when only some users need the advanced features. Downgrading users who don't use E5 features can save ¥2,500/user/month.

Eliminating duplicate services: If you have both Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, standardize on one. Paying for both is wasteful.

Optimizing add-ons: Review all add-ons to ensure they're being used. Unused add-ons should be cancelled.

Annual vs. monthly billing: Annual billing is typically 15-20% cheaper than monthly. If cash flow permits, switch to annual.

SaaS Management: The Growing Challenge

Cloud software creates new SAM challenges.

Shadow SaaS Discovery

Finding cloud services that users have signed up for:

Cloud access security brokers (CASBs): Tools like Microsoft Cloud App Security, Netskope, or McAfee MVISION Cloud can identify cloud services being accessed from your network.

Expense report analysis: Review expense reports for software subscriptions. Users may be expensing SaaS subscriptions that IT doesn't know about.

Email domain monitoring: Monitor for corporate email addresses being used to sign up for cloud services. Many services send confirmation emails that can be detected.

Financial audits: Regular audits of corporate credit card statements can reveal SaaS subscriptions.

Subscription Tracking and Renewal Management

Managing ongoing SaaS subscriptions:

Central tracking: Maintain a central inventory of all SaaS subscriptions including cost, renewal date, owner, and business justification.

Renewal alerts: Set alerts 30-60 days before renewals so you can evaluate whether to continue, modify, or cancel.

Usage reviews: Regularly review whether SaaS services are being used. Low usage may indicate the service isn't needed.

Contract consolidation: Where possible, consolidate SaaS contracts to improve negotiating position and simplify management.

Usage Analytics and Optimization

Understanding how SaaS is being used:

Built-in analytics: Most SaaS platforms provide usage analytics. Review these to understand actual usage patterns.

License tier optimization: Many SaaS services have multiple tiers. Ensure users are on appropriate tiers based on their usage.

Seat optimization: Remove users who aren't using the service. Many organizations pay for "seats" that are never used.

Security and Access Control

Ensuring SaaS services are secure:

Single sign-on (SSO): Where possible, use SSO rather than separate passwords for each service. This improves security and simplifies user management.

Multi-factor authentication: Require MFA for all SaaS services, especially those containing sensitive data.

Access reviews: Regularly review who has access to what SaaS services. Remove access for users who no longer need it.

Integration with Identity Management

Connecting SaaS to your identity infrastructure:

Directory integration: Integrate SaaS services with your directory (Azure AD, Okta, etc.) for centralized user management.

Automated provisioning: Use automated provisioning to create and remove accounts based on directory membership. This ensures that when employees leave, their SaaS access is automatically terminated.

Role-based access: Implement role-based access control so users get appropriate access based on their job function.

SAM Tools for Japan-Based Organizations

Tools can significantly improve SAM effectiveness.

Global Solutions

Flexera: Enterprise-grade SAM with strong discovery, normalization, and optimization capabilities. Good for large organizations with complex environments.

Snow Software: Comprehensive SAM platform with strong cloud service management. Good for organizations with significant cloud software usage.

ServiceNow: ITSM platform with SAM capabilities integrated into broader IT management. Good for organizations already using ServiceNow for ITSM.

Microsoft-Specific Tools

Microsoft 365 Admin Center: Built-in tools for managing Microsoft 365 licenses, including usage reports and license assignment.

Azure AD: Identity management with reporting on application usage and access.

Microsoft Cloud App Security: CASB for discovering and managing cloud services.

Considerations for Mixed JP/EN Environments

For international companies in Japan:

Multi-language support: Ensure SAM tools support both Japanese and English for user interfaces, reports, and documentation.

Local integration: Consider whether tools can integrate with Japanese business systems like accounting software or HR systems.

Support availability: Ensure vendors provide support in appropriate languages and time zones for your needs.

Cost-Benefit Analysis

SAM tools represent an investment:

Tool costs: Enterprise SAM tools can cost ¥1-5 million annually depending on organization size. This needs to be weighed against expected savings.

Implementation costs: Implementing SAM tools requires time and effort. Factor in implementation costs when evaluating ROI.

Ongoing effort: Tools require ongoing maintenance and management. Ensure you have resources to maintain the tool effectively.

DIY vs. tool: For smaller organizations, spreadsheet-based SAM may be sufficient. As you grow, tools become more valuable.

---

About AKRIN

AKRIN K.K. is a Tokyo-based managed IT services company founded in 2024, providing software asset management services for international companies operating in Japan. We help organizations optimize their Microsoft 365 licensing, manage SaaS subscriptions, and maintain compliance with software vendor requirements. Our bilingual team understands both global software licensing and Japan-specific considerations. Contact us for a free SAM assessment.
